addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Orient yourself and lets talk about making those fixes.

content.content: (mcp__vvx__jjx completed with no output)

1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BH
2	
3	## Premises
4	
5	This section exists so a returning session can re-orient against the *why* of the model, not just the *what*. The choices below are load-bearing; understanding them is necessary to keep follow-up paces aligned with the heat's intent.
6	
7	**Why this heat exists.** Three capture needs share gestalt: today's narrow base enshrinement, WSL substrate (the admin-distro replacement needed to take caparison-w off Microsoft's mutable appx supply), and podman VM (defensive mirror against quay's hostile lifecycle — new images every few hours, retention measured in days). The rule-of-three test made verb-generalization load-bearing rather than premature abstraction. This revision absorbed a fourth case — today's reliquary "mirror" operation — because its gesture (pull upstream → SLSA-wrap → store in GAR) is identical to the others. Refusing to unify it once the three-kinds story was honest would have been arbitrary.
8	
9	**Why "Lode" earns the universal recipe role.** All four+ capture cases share one verb shape. The realistic alternative was parallel per-kind infrastructure with parallel vocabulary per kind; unification is the actual simplification, not a forced one. Kinds carry the cardinality information (atomic 1:1 vs compound 1:N for the fan-out cases), so collapsing to one recipe noun loses no precision — the kind enum holds what the parallel vocabulary would have.
10	
11	**Why the Vessel:Lode :: Hallmark:{INSTANCE_NOUN} analogy is load-bearing.** It is the orientation handle for design questions not yet in the paddock. When a future pace asks "how should operation X work for Lodes?", the answer is usually "find X for Vessels and transfer." The instance-noun's lexical choice is open; the structural role is locked. If a future session is tempted to break the analogy because it feels asymmetric, that asymmetry is almost certainly a sign the question is being asked wrong.
12	
13	**Why the .env carries no FQIN — most regression-prone principle.** When quay or docker-hub reorganizes upstream paths, kind-pipeline code changes; .env files do not. If a future pace proposes adding resolved-coordinate detail to a Lode .env, that is a smell — the principle is being violated. RBSRV lines 119-144 are the existing precedent: ORIGIN as author intent, ANCHOR as resolved-by-pipeline. The Lode model generalizes that pattern, not weakens it. Each kind's upstream-source convention lives in pipeline code where it can be fixed in one place.
14	
15	**Why reliquary survives as a kind but not as a top-level noun.** Vessels constrain Cloud Build toolsets by date-cohort identity; the cohort concept itself is empirically load-bearing — removing it would dissolve a real capability. But the project's cult-vocabulary count is high, and reductions are precious. Demotion to a single kind enum slot preserves the cohort semantic while dropping one top-level noun. Keep the word, drop the weight. An earlier framing of this revision flirted with promoting Reliquary to a universal grouping abstraction; that was rejected as wrong-direction because it made a cult noun *more* central, not less.
16	
17	**Why six kinds, not four.** `rbv_PodmanVM.sh` revealed that podman-vm fan-out is two-family at quay (`machine-os-wsl` for the Windows-via-WSL host path, `machine-os` for the macOS/Linux native host path) with asymmetric host-platform consumer paths (caparison-w for the WSL flavor; nothing analogous for native). Splitting honors structural truth, not classification preference. The `tool` and `reliquary` kinds enter because mirror absorption required them — `tool` is the atomic per-image case, `reliquary` is the date-cohort compound.
18	
19	**Why podvm kinds retain selectively, not in bulk.** The upstream manifest lists at quay span 5-15 GB across all host platforms (architecture × disktype × family). Project storage cost scales only to selected variants — a compound Lode's manifest *is* the curated subset. The existing `rbv_PodmanVM.sh` already commits to this shape via `crane manifest` and `crane blob` digest navigation; the model preserves it. Trade-off worth knowing: widen the captured platform set defensively at enshrine time, because quay's hostile retention (new images every few hours, days of retention) can make later expansion impossible once an upstream version has aged out. The pipeline should support "expand selected set against same upstream version" as a first-class refresh mode, separate from "bump to new podman version." A future session may be tempted to regress toward full-mirror out of a misguided fidelity instinct ("we mirror what quay shipped") — resist: cohort identity is project-controlled, vouching attests to the curated subset, and per-platform reproducibility from upstream digests is preserved either way.
20	
21	**Why generator expressions, not enumerations.** A list of "verbs affected" or "files to change" written today is wrong tomorrow — every unrelated touch ages it. A discovery recipe ("scan RBSA*.adoc and RBSI*.adoc for verbs touching captured artifacts") survives those churns. Future mount sessions should resist the urge to expand recipes into lists until paces actually approach the work. Paddock complexity compounds, and complex paddocks become unread — which defeats their purpose. Treat this as discipline, not preference.
22	
23	## Shape
24	
25	Generalize today's narrow `enshrine` (base OCI mirror) into a universal verb for project-controlled capture of upstream artifacts. The verb spans heterogeneous content kinds — base images, build-time tools, WSL substrate, and podman machine images — under a single recipe noun, **Lode**, paralleling Vessel on the built side.
26	
27	The analogy:
28	
29	| Built side | Captured side |
30	|-----------|---------------|
31	| Vessel (recipe) | Lode (recipe) |
32	| Hallmark (specific build) | {INSTANCE_NOUN} (specific capture) |
33	
34	A Lode declares author intent (kind + kind-specific primitives + sigil); the enshrine pipeline resolves upstream coordinates from kind conventions, captures bytes into GAR, and emits a resolved {INSTANCE_NOUN} that consumers reference. Lode .env stays declarative-only — no FQIN field, no resolved-identity field. Kindle code computes upstream FQINs and GAR tags from primitives at module-startup, mirroring the existing `ZRBV_VMIMAGE_TAG_PREFIX` pattern in `rbv_PodmanVM.sh`.
35	
36	Variable groups within the Lode regime gate on the kind enum exactly as RBRV's groups gate on `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE`. RBRV's existing ORIGIN/ANCHOR pattern (RBSRV lines 119-144) — author declares ORIGIN intent, foundry resolves and writes back ANCHOR — is the universal precedent the Lode model generalizes: Lode .env carries author intent only; enshrine writes the resolved capture identity to wherever consumers read it.
37	
38	## Kinds
39	
40	| Kind | Atomic / Compound | Upstream source |
41	|------|-------------------|-----------------|
42	| `base` | atomic | upstream OCI registry, consumed as FROM line |
43	| `tool` | atomic | upstream OCI registry, consumed at build time |
44	| `reliquary` | compound | date-cohort of `tool` sub-Lodes (replaces today's reliquary noun) |
45	| `wsl` | atomic | rootfs tar from Microsoft Store distro via `wsl --export` |
46	| `podvm-wsl` | compound | `quay.io/podman/machine-os-wsl` — platform fan-out, consumed by Windows podman |
47	| `podvm-native` | compound | `quay.io/podman/machine-os` — platform fan-out, consumed by macOS/Linux podman |
48	
49	Compound Lodes hold a manifest of sub-Lode references; sub-Lodes are first-class addressable (each has its own {INSTANCE_NOUN}) but the compound is the typical reference target.
50	
51	`wsl` and `podvm-wsl` both touch WSL but are entirely distinct: `wsl` is the Linux distro tarball that hosts everything on the Windows workload, `podvm-wsl` is the podman machine image that runs *inside* that distro. Names kept visibly different.
52	
53	## Revised from prior paddock
54	
55	- Three kinds (base / wsl / podman-vm) → six kinds. `tool` and `reliquary` enter scope (subsume today's reliquary-as-noun and the mirror verb); `podman-vm` splits into `podvm-wsl` and `podvm-native` to honor the structural asymmetry confirmed by `rbv_PodmanVM.sh`.
56	- Sibling specs per kind → unified Lode regime with kind-dispatched pipelines (mirrors RBRV's enum-gated-groups pattern).
57	- Sibling tabtargets `rbw-dEb/w/v` → leaning toward unified `rbw-dE` with kind argument; pending firm decision.
58	- Single-layer OCI universal wire format survives, but compound Lodes carry a manifest-of-references payload rather than a single payload byte-stream.
59	
60	## Carried forward from prior paddock
61	
62	- Caparison-w / garrison-w consequences for `wsl` kind: admin's WSL distribution disappears when the kind lands; the pre-existing revert pace in ₣A- targeting the WSL-stage DEV CACHE shortcut becomes obsolete and wants dropping or transferring when this heat commits to development.
63	- Refresh cadence variability (now per-kind, may diverge between `podvm-wsl` and `podvm-native`).
64	- "Mirror" verb retires entirely; absorbed into enshrine + `tool` / `reliquary` kinds.
65	
66	## Discovery recipes — work to enumerate when paces approach
67	
68	- **Verb redesign blast radius unaudited.** Discovery recipe: scan `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSA*.adoc` and `RBSI*.adoc` for verbs touching captured artifacts; for each, classify per-kind variance (unchanged / split / retired). Yoke is the headline because consumer-landing target differs per kind; other splits expected but uncatalogued. Keeping this as a recipe rather than an enumeration is deliberate — premature listing inflates the paddock and ages poorly.
69	- **Non-vessel consumer landing regime.** For `wsl` and `podvm-*` kinds, the resolved {INSTANCE_NOUN} has no vessel rbrv.env to yoke into. Decision deferred until the first non-vessel Lode pace mounts and forces the choice; candidates include station-regime extensions, a Lode-side current-resolution field, or a new host-config regime.
70	- **Spec letter allocation for the new Lode spec.** Consult CLAUDE.md Prefix Naming Discipline and Quoin Sub-Letter Discipline before minting; check terminal exclusivity against the existing RBS* tree.
71	
72	## Vocabulary parked
73	
74	- **Lode** chosen as the universal recipe noun. **Unction** held as the strongest rejected alternative (religious register, RBRU_ free, but verb-coupled meaning). **Oblate** rejected — RBRO_ collides with OAuth regime.
75	- **Reliquary** demoted from top-level noun to a kind enum value. Word survives; cult-vocabulary weight reduced.
76	- **{INSTANCE_NOUN}** (the specific-capture noun, parallel to Hallmark) is open. Letter-collision constraint with established project nouns leaves few unused letters; review needed before naming. Anchor was floated as the natural-preserving choice but flagged as uncertain.
77	- Three cohort options considered: α Reliquary-as-grouping abstraction, β Compound Lode, γ no grouping concept. γ rejected by date-cohort load-bearing test (vessels constrain Cloud Build toolsets by cohort); α rejected as wrong-direction (promotes a cult noun rather than reducing); β chosen.
78	
79	## Heat nature
80	
81	Planning/design heat — no paces yet. Paddock encodes the model and the recipes for follow-up work; specific verb-fate, landing-regime, and instance-noun decisions defer until paces commit. Heat silks (`rbk-29-win-enshrine-triple`) now mismatches scope and wants relabeling once the model is approved.
82	
83	## References
84	
85	- ₣AV `rbw-implement-gar-mirroring` (retired 260511) — predecessor heat
86	- RBSAE `ark_enshrine` — current narrow-scope spec; becomes the `base`-kind pipeline under unification
87	- RBSAS `ark_summon`, RBSDI `depot_inscribe`, RBSDY `director_yoke` — verbs within the blast radius
88	- RBSRV `RegimeVessel` (lines 119-144 in particular) — ORIGIN/ANCHOR precedent; enum-gated-variable-groups pattern is the structural model
89	- `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/FUTURE/rbv_PodmanVM.sh` — concrete podman-vm pipeline; reveals fan-out structure and two-source quay split
90	- BUSJCW CaparisonWindows, BUSJGW GarrisonWsl, BUSJIW InvigilateWindows — `wsl` kind consequences on Windows workload
91	- CLAUDE.md "Prefix Naming Discipline" and "Quoin Sub-Letter Discipline" — guide spec letter allocation
92

# jjezs_paddock ₣BH

## Premises

This section exists so a returning session can re-orient against the *why* of the model, not just the *what*. The choices below are load-bearing; understanding them is necessary to keep follow-up paces aligned with the heat's intent.

**Why this heat exists.** Three capture needs share gestalt: today's narrow base enshrinement, WSL substrate (the admin-distro replacement needed to take caparison-w off Microsoft's mutable appx supply), and podman VM (defensive mirror against quay's hostile lifecycle — new images every few hours, retention measured in days). The rule-of-three test made verb-generalization load-bearing rather than premature abstraction. This revision absorbed a fourth case — today's reliquary "mirror" operation — because its gesture (pull upstream → SLSA-wrap → store in GAR) is identical to the others. Refusing to unify it once the three-kinds story was honest would have been arbitrary.

**Why "Lode" earns the universal recipe role.** All four+ capture cases share one verb shape. The realistic alternative was parallel per-kind infrastructure with parallel vocabulary per kind; unification is the actual simplification, not a forced one. Kinds carry the cardinality information (atomic 1:1 vs compound 1:N for the fan-out cases), so collapsing to one recipe noun loses no precision — the kind enum holds what the parallel vocabulary would have.

**Why the Vessel:Lode :: Hallmark:{INSTANCE_NOUN} analogy is load-bearing.** It is the orientation handle for design questions not yet in the paddock. When a future pace asks "how should operation X work for Lodes?", the answer is usually "find X for Vessels and transfer." The instance-noun's lexical choice is open; the structural role is locked. If a future session is tempted to break the analogy because it feels asymmetric, that asymmetry is almost certainly a sign the question is being asked wrong.

**Why the .env carries no FQIN — most regression-prone principle.** When quay or docker-hub reorganizes upstream paths, kind-pipeline code changes; .env files do not. If a future pace proposes adding resolved-coordinate detail to a Lode .env, that is a smell — the principle is being violated. RBSRV lines 119-144 are the existing precedent: ORIGIN as author intent, ANCHOR as resolved-by-pipeline. The Lode model generalizes that pattern, not weakens it. Each kind's upstream-source convention lives in pipeline code where it can be fixed in one place.

**Why reliquary survives as a kind but not as a top-level noun.** Vessels constrain Cloud Build toolsets by date-cohort identity; the cohort concept itself is empirically load-bearing — removing it would dissolve a real capability. But the project's cult-vocabulary count is high, and reductions are precious. Demotion to a single kind enum slot preserves the cohort semantic while dropping one top-level noun. Keep the word, drop the weight. An earlier framing of this revision flirted with promoting Reliquary to a universal grouping abstraction; that was rejected as wrong-direction because it made a cult noun *more* central, not less.

**Why six kinds, not four.** `rbv_PodmanVM.sh` revealed that podman-vm fan-out is two-family at quay (`machine-os-wsl` for the Windows-via-WSL host path, `machine-os` for the macOS/Linux native host path) with asymmetric host-platform consumer paths (caparison-w for the WSL flavor; nothing analogous for native). Splitting honors structural truth, not classification preference. The `tool` and `reliquary` kinds enter because mirror absorption required them — `tool` is the atomic per-image case, `reliquary` is the date-cohort compound.

**Why podvm kinds retain selectively, not in bulk.** The upstream manifest lists at quay span 5-15 GB across all host platforms (architecture × disktype × family). Project storage cost scales only to selected variants — a compound Lode's manifest *is* the curated subset. The existing `rbv_PodmanVM.sh` already commits to this shape via `crane manifest` and `crane blob` digest navigation; the model preserves it. Trade-off worth knowing: widen the captured platform set defensively at enshrine time, because quay's hostile retention (new images every few hours, days of retention) can make later expansion impossible once an upstream version has aged out. The pipeline should support "expand selected set against same upstream version" as a first-class refresh mode, separate from "bump to new podman version." A future session may be tempted to regress toward full-mirror out of a misguided fidelity instinct ("we mirror what quay shipped") — resist: cohort identity is project-controlled, vouching attests to the curated subset, and per-platform reproducibility from upstream digests is preserved either way.

**Why generator expressions, not enumerations.** A list of "verbs affected" or "files to change" written today is wrong tomorrow — every unrelated touch ages it. A discovery recipe ("scan RBSA*.adoc and RBSI*.adoc for verbs touching captured artifacts") survives those churns. Future mount sessions should resist the urge to expand recipes into lists until paces actually approach the work. Paddock complexity compounds, and complex paddocks become unread — which defeats their purpose. Treat this as discipline, not preference.

## Shape

Generalize today's narrow `enshrine` (base OCI mirror) into a universal verb for project-controlled capture of upstream artifacts. The verb spans heterogeneous content kinds — base images, build-time tools, WSL substrate, and podman machine images — under a single recipe noun, **Lode**, paralleling Vessel on the built side.

The analogy:

| Built side | Captured side |
|-----------|---------------|
| Vessel (recipe) | Lode (recipe) |
| Hallmark (specific build) | {INSTANCE_NOUN} (specific capture) |

A Lode declares author intent (kind + kind-specific primitives + sigil); the enshrine pipeline resolves upstream coordinates from kind conventions, captures bytes into GAR, and emits a resolved {INSTANCE_NOUN} that consumers reference. Lode .env stays declarative-only — no FQIN field, no resolved-identity field. Kindle code computes upstream FQINs and GAR tags from primitives at module-startup, mirroring the existing `ZRBV_VMIMAGE_TAG_PREFIX` pattern in `rbv_PodmanVM.sh`.

Variable groups within the Lode regime gate on the kind enum exactly as RBRV's groups gate on `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE`. RBRV's existing ORIGIN/ANCHOR pattern (RBSRV lines 119-144) — author declares ORIGIN intent, foundry resolves and writes back ANCHOR — is the universal precedent the Lode model generalizes: Lode .env carries author intent only; enshrine writes the resolved capture identity to wherever consumers read it.

## Kinds

| Kind | Atomic / Compound | Upstream source |
|------|-------------------|-----------------|
| `base` | atomic | upstream OCI registry, consumed as FROM line |
| `tool` | atomic | upstream OCI registry, consumed at build time |
| `reliquary` | compound | date-cohort of `tool` sub-Lodes (replaces today's reliquary noun) |
| `wsl` | atomic | rootfs tar from Microsoft Store distro via `wsl --export` |
| `podvm-wsl` | compound | `quay.io/podman/machine-os-wsl` — platform fan-out, consumed by Windows podman |
| `podvm-native` | compound | `quay.io/podman/machine-os` — platform fan-out, consumed by macOS/Linux podman |

Compound Lodes hold a manifest of sub-Lode references; sub-Lodes are first-class addressable (each has its own {INSTANCE_NOUN}) but the compound is the typical reference target.

`wsl` and `podvm-wsl` both touch WSL but are entirely distinct: `wsl` is the Linux distro tarball that hosts everything on the Windows workload, `podvm-wsl` is the podman machine image that runs *inside* that distro. Names kept visibly different.

## Revised from prior paddock

- Three kinds (base / wsl / podman-vm) → six kinds. `tool` and `reliquary` enter scope (subsume today's reliquary-as-noun and the mirror verb); `podman-vm` splits into `podvm-wsl` and `podvm-native` to honor the structural asymmetry confirmed by `rbv_PodmanVM.sh`.
- Sibling specs per kind → unified Lode regime with kind-dispatched pipelines (mirrors RBRV's enum-gated-groups pattern).
- Sibling tabtargets `rbw-dEb/w/v` → leaning toward unified `rbw-dE` with kind argument; pending firm decision.
- Single-layer OCI universal wire format survives, but compound Lodes carry a manifest-of-references payload rather than a single payload byte-stream.

## Carried forward from prior paddock

- Caparison-w / garrison-w consequences for `wsl` kind: admin's WSL distribution disappears when the kind lands; the pre-existing revert pace in ₣A- targeting the WSL-stage DEV CACHE shortcut becomes obsolete and wants dropping or transferring when this heat commits to development.
- Refresh cadence variability (now per-kind, may diverge between `podvm-wsl` and `podvm-native`).
- "Mirror" verb retires entirely; absorbed into enshrine + `tool` / `reliquary` kinds.

## Discovery recipes — work to enumerate when paces approach

- **Verb redesign blast radius unaudited.** Discovery recipe: scan `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSA*.adoc` and `RBSI*.adoc` for verbs touching captured artifacts; for each, classify per-kind variance (unchanged / split / retired). Yoke is the headline because consumer-landing target differs per kind; other splits expected but uncatalogued. Keeping this as a recipe rather than an enumeration is deliberate — premature listing inflates the paddock and ages poorly.
- **Non-vessel consumer landing regime.** For `wsl` and `podvm-*` kinds, the resolved {INSTANCE_NOUN} has no vessel rbrv.env to yoke into. Decision deferred until the first non-vessel Lode pace mounts and forces the choice; candidates include station-regime extensions, a Lode-side current-resolution field, or a new host-config regime.
- **Spec letter allocation for the new Lode spec.** Consult CLAUDE.md Prefix Naming Discipline and Quoin Sub-Letter Discipline before minting; check terminal exclusivity against the existing RBS* tree.

## Vocabulary parked

- **Lode** chosen as the universal recipe noun. **Unction** held as the strongest rejected alternative (religious register, RBRU_ free, but verb-coupled meaning). **Oblate** rejected — RBRO_ collides with OAuth regime.
- **Reliquary** demoted from top-level noun to a kind enum value. Word survives; cult-vocabulary weight reduced.
- **{INSTANCE_NOUN}** (the specific-capture noun, parallel to Hallmark) is open. Letter-collision constraint with established project nouns leaves few unused letters; review needed before naming. Anchor was floated as the natural-preserving choice but flagged as uncertain.
- Three cohort options considered: α Reliquary-as-grouping abstraction, β Compound Lode, γ no grouping concept. γ rejected by date-cohort load-bearing test (vessels constrain Cloud Build toolsets by cohort); α rejected as wrong-direction (promotes a cult noun rather than reducing); β chosen.

## Heat nature

Planning/design heat — no paces yet. Paddock encodes the model and the recipes for follow-up work; specific verb-fate, landing-regime, and instance-noun decisions defer until paces commit. Heat silks (`rbk-29-win-enshrine-triple`) now mismatches scope and wants relabeling once the model is approved.

## References

- ₣AV `rbw-implement-gar-mirroring` (retired 260511) — predecessor heat
- RBSAE `ark_enshrine` — current narrow-scope spec; becomes the `base`-kind pipeline under unification
- RBSAS `ark_summon`, RBSDI `depot_inscribe`, RBSDY `director_yoke` — verbs within the blast radius
- RBSRV `RegimeVessel` (lines 119-144 in particular) — ORIGIN/ANCHOR precedent; enum-gated-variable-groups pattern is the structural model
- `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/FUTURE/rbv_PodmanVM.sh` — concrete podman-vm pipeline; reveals fan-out structure and two-source quay split
- BUSJCW CaparisonWindows, BUSJGW GarrisonWsl, BUSJIW InvigilateWindows — `wsl` kind consequences on Windows workload
- CLAUDE.md "Prefix Naming Discipline" and "Quoin Sub-Letter Discipline" — guide spec letter allocation

content.text: Heat oriented. ₣BH is the planning paddock for unifying enshrine into a universal "Lode" capture verb (six kinds: base/tool/reliquary/wsl/podvm-wsl/podvm-native), demoting reliquary from top-level noun, and absorbing the mirror verb. Now let me read the two target files before we talk.

content.content:
1	[[rbshr_horizon_roadmap]]
2	==== Horizon Roadmap
3	
4	Defined-but-unscoped future work across Recipe Bottle.
5	Each item has a rationale (why it matters), a trigger (when to revisit), and a reference (where the evidence lives).
6	
7	Items here are not planned for any heat.
8	When an item becomes actionable, it moves into a heat as paces with proper dockets.
9	This is the single collection point for big changes — individual spec subdocuments should cross-reference here rather than maintaining local "Future Work" sections.
10	
11	**SA numeric ID for member references.**
12	Google recommends using a
13	{giam_service_account}'s
14	numeric ID rather than its email address when referencing it immediately after creation, to reduce propagation delay.
15	Recipe Bottle uses email addresses throughout, which is why propagation retry
16	(<<rbscip_trade_study>>
17	backoff profile) is necessary.
18	Switching to numeric ID could reduce retry frequency but would require threading the numeric ID from SA creation responses through all call chains that construct `serviceAccount:` member strings.
19	Requires verification that `setIamPolicy` accepts numeric ID in member strings.
20	_Trigger_: if propagation retries become a significant wall-clock cost in depot creation or governor reset.
21	_Reference_:
22	<<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>
23	(SA propagation section, evidence table).
24	
25	**Read-back verification universalization.**
26	Currently only
27	{rbtoe_iam_grant_project}
28	performs post-write verification (re-reading policy to confirm the granted role is observable).
29	{rbtoe_iam_grant_repo},
30	{rbtoe_iam_grant_sa},
31	and
32	{rbtoe_iam_grant_bucket}
33	trust the 200 from `setIamPolicy`.
34	Adding verification universally would provide stronger guarantees but adds an HTTP round-trip and a new timeout window per grant.
35	_Trigger_: after etag convergence (\u20a2AsAAF) has been validated in production and we have confidence in the corrected grant contracts.
36	_Reference_:
37	<<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>
38	(Convergence Analysis section).
39	
40	**SA preflight universalization.**
41	Currently only
42	{rbtoe_iam_grant_sa}
43	performs a pre-flight existence check on the target
44	{giam_service_account}.
45	Applying it to all grant types when the member is a service account requires conditional logic and changes behavior for non-SA members.
46	_Trigger_: if grant failures due to non-existent members become a diagnostic problem.
47	_Reference_:
48	<<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>
49	(Convergence Analysis section).
50	
51	**Podman as first-class deployment target.**
52	Recipe Bottle currently deploys to Podman on workstations via the bottle lifecycle (RBSBS, RBSBR, RBSBL).
53	Podman support is functional but not hardened — the sentry container, network isolation, and eBPF enforcement assume Linux Podman with rootful access.
54	Expanding to rootless Podman, Podman on macOS (via podman-machine), or alternative container runtimes would require revisiting the security model and network architecture.
55	_Trigger_: user demand for rootless deployment or macOS-native workflows beyond the current Podman VM approach.
56	_Reference_: RBSPV-PodmanVmSupplyChain.adoc (current VM supply chain);
57	RBSBS, RBSBR, RBSBL (bottle lifecycle specs).
58	
59	**`podman-compose` lacks `--wait` for `compose up`.**
60	Docker Compose `--wait` (v2.1.1+) blocks until all container health checks pass, which `rbob_charge` relies on to guarantee crucible readiness before returning.
61	`podman-compose` does not implement `--wait` — the command returns immediately after scheduling containers, creating a race between charge completion and the active-check assertion in theurge.
62	Without `--wait`, Podman integration will need an alternative readiness gate (polling loop, explicit health-check query, or `podman wait --condition=healthy`).
63	_Trigger_: Podman first-class deployment (above).
64	_Reference_: containers/podman-compose#710 (June 2023, open);
65	containers/podman-compose#1329 (October 2025, open).
66	
67	**VM image supply chain targets GHCR, not GAR.**
68	The Podman VM image pipeline (`rbv_PodmanVM.sh`) mirrors machine-os disk images from quay.io into GHCR as OCI containers, with per-platform tags, brand-file provenance, and crane-based extraction inside an ignite VM.
69	This infrastructure has not been ported to GAR/Depot.
70	Podman deployment requires either migrating the mirror target to GAR or maintaining a parallel GHCR registry alongside the existing Depot project model.
71	_Trigger_: Podman first-class deployment (above).
72	_Reference_: RBSPV-PodmanVmSupplyChain.adoc;
73	`rbv_PodmanVM.sh` (mirror, fetch, init operations).
74	
75	**Hairpin peer access between crucibles.**
76	Bottled containers cannot currently reach each other's published entry ports.
77	Each bottle is isolated behind its own sentry on a separate enclave network.
78	The traffic path is feasible: Bottle A egresses through Sentry A's transit bridge, the host kernel routes to Sentry B's transit bridge, and Sentry B's existing entry path (DNAT + RBM-FORWARD) accepts the connection unchanged.
79	The security model is preserved — the sentry remains the sole enforcement point, with peer access as a new category of allowed destination alongside entry (inbound) and uplink (outbound internet).
80	Nameplate model: two new variables (`RBRN_PEER_ACCESS_MODE` disabled|allowlist, gated `RBRN_PEER_ALLOWED_MONIKERS` as comma-separated moniker list).
81	No global mode — peer access must always be explicit.
82	Implementation touches three areas: nameplate kindle/validate/render, a peer-access iptables step in security_config, and host-side peer IP discovery via container inspection.
83	Audit gains a cross-instance check: each named peer must exist and have entry enabled.
84	_Trigger_: when a use case requires bottle-to-bottle communication (e.g., microservice composition, service mesh patterns).
85	_Reference_: Heat ₣AU paddock (design conversation capture).
86	
87	**Hoard: external artifact mirror in retention-locked GCS.**
88	A
89	{rbtgi_depot}
90	citizen alongside the
91	{rbtga_ark}
92	that mirrors external content-addressed corpora — canonical case: Hugging Face model checkpoints.
93	GCS archive is ~80× cheaper per byte than GAR and supports bucket-level retention locks for compliance-grade immutability that GAR does not offer.
94	Ingress reuses upstream SHA256 addressing (shallow git + LFS pointer resolution, SHA256-keyed GCS objects with natural dedup across revisions);
95	Cloud Build streams so egress stays Google-side;
96	auth path reuses the existing JWT/OAuth bearer curl stack — no new tooling dependency.
97	+
98	Intended consumption: just-in-time workstation mount from the retention-locked bucket into the
99	{at_bottle_container}
100	at charge time, rather than baking models into
101	{at_bottle_image_s}.
102	Keeps images lean, enables model swap without rebuild, keeps the retention property where it naturally lives.
103	Tradeoff: provenance becomes a
104	{at_nameplate}-declared
105	hoard digest recorded at charge time rather than a SLSA-attested image layer — weaker by one link.
106	_Trigger_: first concrete need to run LM inference inside a
107	{at_bottle_container}
108	against a specific HF checkpoint with retention guarantees.
109	_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
110	design conversation pending heat.
111	
112	**CIDR allowlist granularity vs CDN-hosted domains.**
113	The sentry's iptables CIDR rules accept all protocols and all destination ports to allowed IP ranges.
114	This is intentional: the spec defines
115	{rbrn_uplink_access_mode_allowlist}
116	as "IP access restricted to allowed CIDR ranges" — not port-restricted.
117	The DNS allowlist
118	({rbrn_uplink_allowed_domains})
119	provides name-level precision;
120	the CIDR allowlist provides IP-level gating.
121	These two layers are independent and reinforce each other.
122	+
123	The weakness emerges when allowed domains are CDN-hosted.
124	IANA's example.com (RFC 2606) migrated to Cloudflare, requiring CIDR expansion from `93.184.216.0/24` to include `104.16.0.0/12` — a /12 covering ~1M IPs shared with a large fraction of Cloudflare-fronted sites.
125	The DNS allowlist remains precise (two domains), but the CIDR backstop is now porous: any destination within the Cloudflare range is reachable regardless of domain.
126	A compromised or attacker-controlled site sharing the CDN range would be reachable from the bottle.
127	+
128	Three potential mitigations, in order of increasing ambition:
129	+
130	. **Accept as-is with documentation.**
131	  The DNS allowlist is the primary gate;
132	  the CIDR is a coarse backstop.
133	  Document the known weakness for broad CIDRs.
134	  Suitable when the allowed domains are trusted and the CDN range is acceptable collateral exposure.
135	. **Dynamic CIDR from DNS resolution.**
136	  Have dnsmasq feed resolved IPs into iptables at resolution time, so the allowed set tracks actual DNS responses rather than static configuration.
137	  Eliminates CDN range sprawl.
138	  Requires new sentry infrastructure (dnsmasq hook scripts or ipset integration).
139	. **TLS SNI enforcement.**
140	  Inspect TLS ClientHello for the SNI field and enforce that it matches the DNS allowlist.
141	  Closes the CDN gap at the application layer without dynamic iptables.
142	  Requires a transparent TLS proxy or eBPF-based SNI extraction in the sentry — a different order of complexity.
143	+
144	_Trigger_: when allowed domains require CIDRs broader than /24, or when the threat model requires preventing lateral reachability within a CDN range.
145	_Reference_: rbjs_sentry.sh (Phase 3 CIDR rules);
146	.rbk/tadmor/rbrn.env (current CIDR configuration);
147	₣A1 heat ₢A1AAj (discovery context).
148	
149	**Encrypted tunnel conduit (WireGuard in sentry).**
150	Add WireGuard to the sentry image so the sentry terminates an encrypted tunnel directly to a VPC hosting PrivateLink endpoints for cloud services.
151	The bottle's traffic routes through a `wg0` interface on the sentry — one stable VPC CIDR replaces floating cloud-provider IP ranges.
152	WireGuard is ~400KB on Alpine, uses static keypair exchange (no PKI/CA), and fits naturally into the sentry's existing privileged-container, explicit-routing architecture.
153	New nameplate fields: tunnel mode, peer endpoint, public keys, allowed IPs.
154	VPC side requires a small WireGuard peer instance and PrivateLink endpoints for target services.
155	_Trigger_: when a bottle needs to reach a cloud service that supports PrivateLink (AWS Bedrock is the known first case; survey should include Vertex AI, Azure OpenAI, GitLab Dedicated, SageMaker, and others as they emerge).
156	_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (full design exploration, approach comparison, service compatibility survey).
157	
158	**Cloud service conduit nameplate.**
159	A nameplate configuration pattern for bottles that access cloud services at runtime.
160	Near-term: allowlist-only nameplate targeting specific service CIDRs and domains (works today with existing sentry machinery, no development needed).
161	Longer-term: tunnel-mode nameplate using WireGuard conduit (above).
162	AWS Bedrock is the known requirement;
163	the pattern should be researched broadly across cloud AI inference services, managed Git hosting, and other PrivateLink-capable endpoints.
164	_Trigger_: immediate — Bedrock access is a current need.
165	Allowlist-only nameplate can be created now;
166	tunnel-mode nameplate follows WireGuard sentry work.
167	_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (approach A for allowlist-only, approach C for tunnel mode).
168	
169	**Credential confinement to crucible.**
170	Move cloud service credentials (AWS IAM keys, API tokens, SSH keys) from the operator's workstation into the crucible, injected via nameplate/regime configuration.
171	The workstation starts the crucible but never holds service credentials.
172	The existing RBRA file pattern provides a model;
173	extending to tunnel keys and cloud service credentials follows the same regime path approach.
174	Reduces the attack surface from "everything on the workstation" to "one minimal container."
175	_Trigger_: alongside the conduit nameplate work — credentials and network access are naturally paired.
176	_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (Credential Confinement section, threat model table).
177	
178	NOTE: For SaaS services without PrivateLink (GitHub.com, GitLab.com), the sentry CIDR/domain allowlist + TLS remains the realistic ceiling.
179	The sentry still provides the core value: the bottle cannot reach anything the sentry doesn't allow, and the workstation is cut out of the data path.
180	The tunnel adds defense-in-depth only for PrivateLink-capable services.
181	Cross-reference: the existing "CIDR allowlist granularity vs CDN-hosted domains" item (Network Security Model) remains relevant for SaaS-only services where the tunnel cannot help.
182	
183	**Cross-cloud architectural stance: asymmetric sovereignty.**
184	Recipe Bottle governs identities and infrastructure only in GCP.
185	AWS-side presence is shaped as delivered artifacts and policy templates, not owned principals — no AWS-Director, no AWS-Mason, no AWS-Retriever.
186	If Recipe Bottle ever needs to *build* in AWS rather than deliver to it, that would be a deliberate architectural decision triggering a full parallel role hierarchy, not an incremental drift.
187	Vocabulary used in related entries — embassy, envoy, lode, entrust, accredit, recall, chantry — is tentative working language from a design conversation; concepts are firmer than the words.
188	_Trigger_: when AWS-side work moves into a heat with real scope.
189	_Reference_: this roadmap entry; design conversation pending heat.
190	
191	**Embassy — AWS-side delivery premises.**
192	An S3 bucket (with Object Lock in Compliance mode for retention), an IAM role with a trust policy accepting the envoy's federated identity, and Recipe-Bottle-shipped IAM policy shapes that the AWS consumer attaches to whatever principal consumes delivered artifacts (EC2 role, ECS task role, Bedrock execution role, etc.).
193	Provisioned by the payor via operations congruent with
194	{rbtgi_depot}
195	creation — the AWS consumer's embassy is the sovereign-setup equivalent of the Manor's depot.
196	Recipe Bottle ships provisioning templates;
197	the AWS consumer owns the resulting resources.
198	_Trigger_: first concrete need to deliver artifacts to an AWS workload.
199	_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
200	design conversation pending heat.
201	
202	**Envoy — GCP-side cloud-native cross-post actor.**
203	A
204	{giam_service_account}
205	in the depot, sibling to existing cloud-native SAs, whose sole purpose is executing cross-cloud pushes to an accredited embassy.
206	The envoy holds no long-lived AWS credentials;
207	it federates to AWS via OIDC Workload Identity Federation, exchanging short-lived GCP tokens for short-lived AWS STS credentials at operation time.
208	Preserves credential locality: Director holds only GCP credentials on the workstation, envoy holds only federation configuration in GCP, and AWS admin credentials never enter Recipe Bottle's custody.
209	_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
210	_Reference_: this roadmap entry.
211	
212	**Lode — AWS-side content-addressed artifact.**
213	Recipe Bottle's delivered artifact in an embassy bucket: a content-addressed filesystem image (squashfs/erofs, optionally with dm-verity) with Object Lock applied for compliance-grade retention.
214	A lode may carry a vessel image payload (the "Director pushes a vessel to AWS" motivator), model weights, or any other content-addressed corpus.
215	Sibling-but-distinct from the hoard: hoards stay GCS-native and depot-resident, preserving the existing provenance story;
216	lodes are the new AWS-side citizen with S3-native retention semantics.
217	Provenance transports as naming-convention sibling objects (attestation JSON adjacent to the image blob), not as OCI referring artifacts, because the lode is not an OCI artifact.
218	_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
219	_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
220	Artifact Preservation section (hoard entry) for the GCP sibling concept.
221	
222	**Accredit / Recall — governor operations for embassy binding lifecycle.**
223	Accredit: governor configures the envoy's OIDC federation against an embassy's IAM role trust policy, and records the binding (embassy name, AWS account, region, role ARN, bucket name) in depot state.
224	Recall: governor removes the federation configuration and prunes the binding.
225	Follows the existing governor verb register (mantles, charters, knights, forfeits) with diplomatic-register verbs appropriate for foreign-court relationships.
226	A single depot may be accredited to multiple embassies.
227	_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
228	_Reference_: this roadmap entry.
229	
230	**Entrust — director-triggered cross-post operation.**
231	Director invokes entrust on a specific hallmark (or other deliverable) with a target embassy name.
232	The envoy executes the cross-post from within Cloud Build: federates to the embassy's IAM role, streams bytes to S3, applies Object Lock retention.
233	Director holds no AWS credentials;
234	failure modes surface through the existing Cloud Build event stream.
235	_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
236	_Reference_: this roadmap entry.
237	
238	**Chantry — Bedrock-hosted inference endpoint.**
239	Architecturally distinct from the embassy/lode pattern.
240	A chantry is a Bedrock-registered custom model (or a native Bedrock foundation model) invoked by a
241	{at_bottle_container}
242	over the crucible conduit architecture — not an artifact Recipe Bottle delivers, but a foreign-sovereign service a bottle consumes.
243	Weights may originate as a lode (entrusted to an embassy, then consumed by Bedrock Custom Model Import on the AWS side), but the chantry itself is the registered-and-invocable endpoint, not the weight storage.
244	Pairs with the "Cloud service conduit nameplate" entry for the invocation path.
245	_Trigger_: first concrete Bedrock inference requirement;
246	near-term per the Crucible Conduit Architecture section above.
247	_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
248	`Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md`;
249	Crucible Conduit Architecture section above.
250	
251	**Egress lockdown — IMPLEMENTED (₣Av).**
252	Dual-pool architecture: tether pool (public egress) and airgap pool (`NO_PUBLIC_EGRESS`).
253	Operation-level routing: inscribe/enshrine use tether, about/vouch always airgap, conjure/bind per vessel `RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`.
254	Reliquaries (tool images) and anchors (base images) provide universal GAR-sourced dependencies.
255	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Implemented: Egress Lockdown via Dual Pools).
256	
257	**VPC Service Controls perimeter.**
258	Wrap depot project in VPC-SC perimeter restricting API access.
259	Requires egress lockdown (now implemented) as prerequisite, org-level admin for perimeter creation, and verification of every GCP API call in the pipeline.
260	_Trigger_: organizational security policy mandating VPC-SC, or data exfiltration risk assessment.
261	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Potential Hardening: VPC Service Controls).
262	Cross-reference: if a VPC is stood up for the crucible conduit architecture (above), design the VPC-SC perimeter to serve both Cloud Build egress and bottle conduit consumers.
263	
264	**Cosign image signing.**
265	Cryptographic signatures on images, verifiable by downstream consumers.
266	KMS-backed keyed signing preferred for airgap-pool compatibility.
267	Currently redundant given CB-native SLSA v1.0 Build Level 3 attestation.
268	_Trigger_: external image distribution, trust verification needed outside GCP, or builder strategy expanding beyond Google Cloud Build.
269	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Cosign).
270	
271	**Binary Authorization.**
272	Deploy-time policy enforcement (only attested images can be deployed).
273	Technically achievable now with CB-native SLSA Level 3.
274	Not beneficial until deployment targets include GKE or Cloud Run.
275	_Trigger_: deployment targets change from Podman workstations to managed container platforms.
276	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Binary Authorization).
277	
278	**Dockerfile base image digest validation.**
279	Builder step images are digest-pinned, but user-authored Dockerfiles in vessel build contexts are not validated.
280	A vessel with `FROM ubuntu:22.04` uses a mutable tag.
281	Constrains vessel authors (policy decision).
282	_Trigger_: vessel ecosystem matures beyond internal-only use.
283	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Dockerfile base image).
284	
285	**Reproducibility verification.**
286	Periodic independent rebuilds compared by digest.
287	Requires reproducible toolchains — a vessel-author discipline, not a pipeline control.
288	_Trigger_: vessel ecosystem maturity and external distribution.
289	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Reproducibility).
290	
291	**Browser-OAuth human authentication for Governor / Director / Retriever.**
292	Replace SA-keyfile distribution (the RBRA model) with a browser-based OAuth flow that mints short-lived SA tokens via service-account impersonation, eliminating credential artifact distribution.
293	User authenticates once at `accounts.google.com` (no gcloud dependency — reuses the existing curl/openssl/jq stack and the Payor OAuth pattern from RBGP/RBGO);
294	refresh token cached locally;
295	per-operation, the system mints a user access token and calls `iamcredentials.googleapis.com:generateAccessToken` against the target SA to obtain a short-lived SA token.
296	The grant lives in IAM policy on the SA itself (`roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` for `user:alice@example.com`), administered by Governor as a new verb sibling to mantle/charter/knight/forfeit.
297	Distribution dissolves (nothing to ship);
298	revocation becomes one IAM binding deletion or OAuth consent revocation;
299	audit logs gain `delegationInfo` attributing actions to the human behind the SA.
300	Additive in the near term — RBRA keyfiles remain necessary for headless paths (Cloud Build itself, automated fixtures, Ifrit-style scenarios), and a regime mode field selects per-role credential mode.
301	Open questions remain (storage location, OAuth client scope, probe coexistence) — see memo for full design.
302	_Trigger_: first concrete external-user request for browser-based onboarding, or first time keyfile distribution materially blocks adoption rather than being theoretically frictionful.
303	_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260427-google-native-human-auth.md`.
304

file.content:
[[rbshr_horizon_roadmap]]
==== Horizon Roadmap

Defined-but-unscoped future work across Recipe Bottle.
Each item has a rationale (why it matters), a trigger (when to revisit), and a reference (where the evidence lives).

Items here are not planned for any heat.
When an item becomes actionable, it moves into a heat as paces with proper dockets.
This is the single collection point for big changes — individual spec subdocuments should cross-reference here rather than maintaining local "Future Work" sections.

**SA numeric ID for member references.**
Google recommends using a
{giam_service_account}'s
numeric ID rather than its email address when referencing it immediately after creation, to reduce propagation delay.
Recipe Bottle uses email addresses throughout, which is why propagation retry
(<<rbscip_trade_study>>
backoff profile) is necessary.
Switching to numeric ID could reduce retry frequency but would require threading the numeric ID from SA creation responses through all call chains that construct `serviceAccount:` member strings.
Requires verification that `setIamPolicy` accepts numeric ID in member strings.
_Trigger_: if propagation retries become a significant wall-clock cost in depot creation or governor reset.
_Reference_:
<<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>
(SA propagation section, evidence table).

**Read-back verification universalization.**
Currently only
{rbtoe_iam_grant_project}
performs post-write verification (re-reading policy to confirm the granted role is observable).
{rbtoe_iam_grant_repo},
{rbtoe_iam_grant_sa},
and
{rbtoe_iam_grant_bucket}
trust the 200 from `setIamPolicy`.
Adding verification universally would provide stronger guarantees but adds an HTTP round-trip and a new timeout window per grant.
_Trigger_: after etag convergence (\u20a2AsAAF) has been validated in production and we have confidence in the corrected grant contracts.
_Reference_:
<<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>
(Convergence Analysis section).

**SA preflight universalization.**
Currently only
{rbtoe_iam_grant_sa}
performs a pre-flight existence check on the target
{giam_service_account}.
Applying it to all grant types when the member is a service account requires conditional logic and changes behavior for non-SA members.
_Trigger_: if grant failures due to non-existent members become a diagnostic problem.
_Reference_:
<<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>
(Convergence Analysis section).

**Podman as first-class deployment target.**
Recipe Bottle currently deploys to Podman on workstations via the bottle lifecycle (RBSBS, RBSBR, RBSBL).
Podman support is functional but not hardened — the sentry container, network isolation, and eBPF enforcement assume Linux Podman with rootful access.
Expanding to rootless Podman, Podman on macOS (via podman-machine), or alternative container runtimes would require revisiting the security model and network architecture.
_Trigger_: user demand for rootless deployment or macOS-native workflows beyond the current Podman VM approach.
_Reference_: RBSPV-PodmanVmSupplyChain.adoc (current VM supply chain);
RBSBS, RBSBR, RBSBL (bottle lifecycle specs).

**`podman-compose` lacks `--wait` for `compose up`.**
Docker Compose `--wait` (v2.1.1+) blocks until all container health checks pass, which `rbob_charge` relies on to guarantee crucible readiness before returning.
`podman-compose` does not implement `--wait` — the command returns immediately after scheduling containers, creating a race between charge completion and the active-check assertion in theurge.
Without `--wait`, Podman integration will need an alternative readiness gate (polling loop, explicit health-check query, or `podman wait --condition=healthy`).
_Trigger_: Podman first-class deployment (above).
_Reference_: containers/podman-compose#710 (June 2023, open);
containers/podman-compose#1329 (October 2025, open).

**VM image supply chain targets GHCR, not GAR.**
The Podman VM image pipeline (`rbv_PodmanVM.sh`) mirrors machine-os disk images from quay.io into GHCR as OCI containers, with per-platform tags, brand-file provenance, and crane-based extraction inside an ignite VM.
This infrastructure has not been ported to GAR/Depot.
Podman deployment requires either migrating the mirror target to GAR or maintaining a parallel GHCR registry alongside the existing Depot project model.
_Trigger_: Podman first-class deployment (above).
_Reference_: RBSPV-PodmanVmSupplyChain.adoc;
`rbv_PodmanVM.sh` (mirror, fetch, init operations).

**Hairpin peer access between crucibles.**
Bottled containers cannot currently reach each other's published entry ports.
Each bottle is isolated behind its own sentry on a separate enclave network.
The traffic path is feasible: Bottle A egresses through Sentry A's transit bridge, the host kernel routes to Sentry B's transit bridge, and Sentry B's existing entry path (DNAT + RBM-FORWARD) accepts the connection unchanged.
The security model is preserved — the sentry remains the sole enforcement point, with peer access as a new category of allowed destination alongside entry (inbound) and uplink (outbound internet).
Nameplate model: two new variables (`RBRN_PEER_ACCESS_MODE` disabled|allowlist, gated `RBRN_PEER_ALLOWED_MONIKERS` as comma-separated moniker list).
No global mode — peer access must always be explicit.
Implementation touches three areas: nameplate kindle/validate/render, a peer-access iptables step in security_config, and host-side peer IP discovery via container inspection.
Audit gains a cross-instance check: each named peer must exist and have entry enabled.
_Trigger_: when a use case requires bottle-to-bottle communication (e.g., microservice composition, service mesh patterns).
_Reference_: Heat ₣AU paddock (design conversation capture).

**Hoard: external artifact mirror in retention-locked GCS.**
A
{rbtgi_depot}
citizen alongside the
{rbtga_ark}
that mirrors external content-addressed corpora — canonical case: Hugging Face model checkpoints.
GCS archive is ~80× cheaper per byte than GAR and supports bucket-level retention locks for compliance-grade immutability that GAR does not offer.
Ingress reuses upstream SHA256 addressing (shallow git + LFS pointer resolution, SHA256-keyed GCS objects with natural dedup across revisions);
Cloud Build streams so egress stays Google-side;
auth path reuses the existing JWT/OAuth bearer curl stack — no new tooling dependency.
+
Intended consumption: just-in-time workstation mount from the retention-locked bucket into the
{at_bottle_container}
at charge time, rather than baking models into
{at_bottle_image_s}.
Keeps images lean, enables model swap without rebuild, keeps the retention property where it naturally lives.
Tradeoff: provenance becomes a
{at_nameplate}-declared
hoard digest recorded at charge time rather than a SLSA-attested image layer — weaker by one link.
_Trigger_: first concrete need to run LM inference inside a
{at_bottle_container}
against a specific HF checkpoint with retention guarantees.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
design conversation pending heat.

**CIDR allowlist granularity vs CDN-hosted domains.**
The sentry's iptables CIDR rules accept all protocols and all destination ports to allowed IP ranges.
This is intentional: the spec defines
{rbrn_uplink_access_mode_allowlist}
as "IP access restricted to allowed CIDR ranges" — not port-restricted.
The DNS allowlist
({rbrn_uplink_allowed_domains})
provides name-level precision;
the CIDR allowlist provides IP-level gating.
These two layers are independent and reinforce each other.
+
The weakness emerges when allowed domains are CDN-hosted.
IANA's example.com (RFC 2606) migrated to Cloudflare, requiring CIDR expansion from `93.184.216.0/24` to include `104.16.0.0/12` — a /12 covering ~1M IPs shared with a large fraction of Cloudflare-fronted sites.
The DNS allowlist remains precise (two domains), but the CIDR backstop is now porous: any destination within the Cloudflare range is reachable regardless of domain.
A compromised or attacker-controlled site sharing the CDN range would be reachable from the bottle.
+
Three potential mitigations, in order of increasing ambition:
+
. **Accept as-is with documentation.**
  The DNS allowlist is the primary gate;
  the CIDR is a coarse backstop.
  Document the known weakness for broad CIDRs.
  Suitable when the allowed domains are trusted and the CDN range is acceptable collateral exposure.
. **Dynamic CIDR from DNS resolution.**
  Have dnsmasq feed resolved IPs into iptables at resolution time, so the allowed set tracks actual DNS responses rather than static configuration.
  Eliminates CDN range sprawl.
  Requires new sentry infrastructure (dnsmasq hook scripts or ipset integration).
. **TLS SNI enforcement.**
  Inspect TLS ClientHello for the SNI field and enforce that it matches the DNS allowlist.
  Closes the CDN gap at the application layer without dynamic iptables.
  Requires a transparent TLS proxy or eBPF-based SNI extraction in the sentry — a different order of complexity.
+
_Trigger_: when allowed domains require CIDRs broader than /24, or when the threat model requires preventing lateral reachability within a CDN range.
_Reference_: rbjs_sentry.sh (Phase 3 CIDR rules);
.rbk/tadmor/rbrn.env (current CIDR configuration);
₣A1 heat ₢A1AAj (discovery context).

**Encrypted tunnel conduit (WireGuard in sentry).**
Add WireGuard to the sentry image so the sentry terminates an encrypted tunnel directly to a VPC hosting PrivateLink endpoints for cloud services.
The bottle's traffic routes through a `wg0` interface on the sentry — one stable VPC CIDR replaces floating cloud-provider IP ranges.
WireGuard is ~400KB on Alpine, uses static keypair exchange (no PKI/CA), and fits naturally into the sentry's existing privileged-container, explicit-routing architecture.
New nameplate fields: tunnel mode, peer endpoint, public keys, allowed IPs.
VPC side requires a small WireGuard peer instance and PrivateLink endpoints for target services.
_Trigger_: when a bottle needs to reach a cloud service that supports PrivateLink (AWS Bedrock is the known first case; survey should include Vertex AI, Azure OpenAI, GitLab Dedicated, SageMaker, and others as they emerge).
_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (full design exploration, approach comparison, service compatibility survey).

**Cloud service conduit nameplate.**
A nameplate configuration pattern for bottles that access cloud services at runtime.
Near-term: allowlist-only nameplate targeting specific service CIDRs and domains (works today with existing sentry machinery, no development needed).
Longer-term: tunnel-mode nameplate using WireGuard conduit (above).
AWS Bedrock is the known requirement;
the pattern should be researched broadly across cloud AI inference services, managed Git hosting, and other PrivateLink-capable endpoints.
_Trigger_: immediate — Bedrock access is a current need.
Allowlist-only nameplate can be created now;
tunnel-mode nameplate follows WireGuard sentry work.
_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (approach A for allowlist-only, approach C for tunnel mode).

**Credential confinement to crucible.**
Move cloud service credentials (AWS IAM keys, API tokens, SSH keys) from the operator's workstation into the crucible, injected via nameplate/regime configuration.
The workstation starts the crucible but never holds service credentials.
The existing RBRA file pattern provides a model;
extending to tunnel keys and cloud service credentials follows the same regime path approach.
Reduces the attack surface from "everything on the workstation" to "one minimal container."
_Trigger_: alongside the conduit nameplate work — credentials and network access are naturally paired.
_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (Credential Confinement section, threat model table).

NOTE: For SaaS services without PrivateLink (GitHub.com, GitLab.com), the sentry CIDR/domain allowlist + TLS remains the realistic ceiling.
The sentry still provides the core value: the bottle cannot reach anything the sentry doesn't allow, and the workstation is cut out of the data path.
The tunnel adds defense-in-depth only for PrivateLink-capable services.
Cross-reference: the existing "CIDR allowlist granularity vs CDN-hosted domains" item (Network Security Model) remains relevant for SaaS-only services where the tunnel cannot help.

**Cross-cloud architectural stance: asymmetric sovereignty.**
Recipe Bottle governs identities and infrastructure only in GCP.
AWS-side presence is shaped as delivered artifacts and policy templates, not owned principals — no AWS-Director, no AWS-Mason, no AWS-Retriever.
If Recipe Bottle ever needs to *build* in AWS rather than deliver to it, that would be a deliberate architectural decision triggering a full parallel role hierarchy, not an incremental drift.
Vocabulary used in related entries — embassy, envoy, lode, entrust, accredit, recall, chantry — is tentative working language from a design conversation; concepts are firmer than the words.
_Trigger_: when AWS-side work moves into a heat with real scope.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry; design conversation pending heat.

**Embassy — AWS-side delivery premises.**
An S3 bucket (with Object Lock in Compliance mode for retention), an IAM role with a trust policy accepting the envoy's federated identity, and Recipe-Bottle-shipped IAM policy shapes that the AWS consumer attaches to whatever principal consumes delivered artifacts (EC2 role, ECS task role, Bedrock execution role, etc.).
Provisioned by the payor via operations congruent with
{rbtgi_depot}
creation — the AWS consumer's embassy is the sovereign-setup equivalent of the Manor's depot.
Recipe Bottle ships provisioning templates;
the AWS consumer owns the resulting resources.
_Trigger_: first concrete need to deliver artifacts to an AWS workload.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
design conversation pending heat.

**Envoy — GCP-side cloud-native cross-post actor.**
A
{giam_service_account}
in the depot, sibling to existing cloud-native SAs, whose sole purpose is executing cross-cloud pushes to an accredited embassy.
The envoy holds no long-lived AWS credentials;
it federates to AWS via OIDC Workload Identity Federation, exchanging short-lived GCP tokens for short-lived AWS STS credentials at operation time.
Preserves credential locality: Director holds only GCP credentials on the workstation, envoy holds only federation configuration in GCP, and AWS admin credentials never enter Recipe Bottle's custody.
_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry.

**Lode — AWS-side content-addressed artifact.**
Recipe Bottle's delivered artifact in an embassy bucket: a content-addressed filesystem image (squashfs/erofs, optionally with dm-verity) with Object Lock applied for compliance-grade retention.
A lode may carry a vessel image payload (the "Director pushes a vessel to AWS" motivator), model weights, or any other content-addressed corpus.
Sibling-but-distinct from the hoard: hoards stay GCS-native and depot-resident, preserving the existing provenance story;
lodes are the new AWS-side citizen with S3-native retention semantics.
Provenance transports as naming-convention sibling objects (attestation JSON adjacent to the image blob), not as OCI referring artifacts, because the lode is not an OCI artifact.
_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
Artifact Preservation section (hoard entry) for the GCP sibling concept.

**Accredit / Recall — governor operations for embassy binding lifecycle.**
Accredit: governor configures the envoy's OIDC federation against an embassy's IAM role trust policy, and records the binding (embassy name, AWS account, region, role ARN, bucket name) in depot state.
Recall: governor removes the federation configuration and prunes the binding.
Follows the existing governor verb register (mantles, charters, knights, forfeits) with diplomatic-register verbs appropriate for foreign-court relationships.
A single depot may be accredited to multiple embassies.
_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry.

**Entrust — director-triggered cross-post operation.**
Director invokes entrust on a specific hallmark (or other deliverable) with a target embassy name.
The envoy executes the cross-post from within Cloud Build: federates to the embassy's IAM role, streams bytes to S3, applies Object Lock retention.
Director holds no AWS credentials;
failure modes surface through the existing Cloud Build event stream.
_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry.

**Chantry — Bedrock-hosted inference endpoint.**
Architecturally distinct from the embassy/lode pattern.
A chantry is a Bedrock-registered custom model (or a native Bedrock foundation model) invoked by a
{at_bottle_container}
over the crucible conduit architecture — not an artifact Recipe Bottle delivers, but a foreign-sovereign service a bottle consumes.
Weights may originate as a lode (entrusted to an embassy, then consumed by Bedrock Custom Model Import on the AWS side), but the chantry itself is the registered-and-invocable endpoint, not the weight storage.
Pairs with the "Cloud service conduit nameplate" entry for the invocation path.
_Trigger_: first concrete Bedrock inference requirement;
near-term per the Crucible Conduit Architecture section above.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
`Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md`;
Crucible Conduit Architecture section above.

**Egress lockdown — IMPLEMENTED (₣Av).**
Dual-pool architecture: tether pool (public egress) and airgap pool (`NO_PUBLIC_EGRESS`).
Operation-level routing: inscribe/enshrine use tether, about/vouch always airgap, conjure/bind per vessel `RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`.
Reliquaries (tool images) and anchors (base images) provide universal GAR-sourced dependencies.
_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Implemented: Egress Lockdown via Dual Pools).

**VPC Service Controls perimeter.**
Wrap depot project in VPC-SC perimeter restricting API access.
Requires egress lockdown (now implemented) as prerequisite, org-level admin for perimeter creation, and verification of every GCP API call in the pipeline.
_Trigger_: organizational security policy mandating VPC-SC, or data exfiltration risk assessment.
_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Potential Hardening: VPC Service Controls).
Cross-reference: if a VPC is stood up for the crucible conduit architecture (above), design the VPC-SC perimeter to serve both Cloud Build egress and bottle conduit consumers.

**Cosign image signing.**
Cryptographic signatures on images, verifiable by downstream consumers.
KMS-backed keyed signing preferred for airgap-pool compatibility.
Currently redundant given CB-native SLSA v1.0 Build Level 3 attestation.
_Trigger_: external image distribution, trust verification needed outside GCP, or builder strategy expanding beyond Google Cloud Build.
_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Cosign).

**Binary Authorization.**
Deploy-time policy enforcement (only attested images can be deployed).
Technically achievable now with CB-native SLSA Level 3.
Not beneficial until deployment targets include GKE or Cloud Run.
_Trigger_: deployment targets change from Podman workstations to managed container platforms.
_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Binary Authorization).

**Dockerfile base image digest validation.**
Builder step images are digest-pinned, but user-authored Dockerfiles in vessel build contexts are not validated.
A vessel with `FROM ubuntu:22.04` uses a mutable tag.
Constrains vessel authors (policy decision).
_Trigger_: vessel ecosystem matures beyond internal-only use.
_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Dockerfile base image).

**Reproducibility verification.**
Periodic independent rebuilds compared by digest.
Requires reproducible toolchains — a vessel-author discipline, not a pipeline control.
_Trigger_: vessel ecosystem maturity and external distribution.
_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Reproducibility).

**Browser-OAuth human authentication for Governor / Director / Retriever.**
Replace SA-keyfile distribution (the RBRA model) with a browser-based OAuth flow that mints short-lived SA tokens via service-account impersonation, eliminating credential artifact distribution.
User authenticates once at `accounts.google.com` (no gcloud dependency — reuses the existing curl/openssl/jq stack and the Payor OAuth pattern from RBGP/RBGO);
refresh token cached locally;
per-operation, the system mints a user access token and calls `iamcredentials.googleapis.com:generateAccessToken` against the target SA to obtain a short-lived SA token.
The grant lives in IAM policy on the SA itself (`roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` for `user:alice@example.com`), administered by Governor as a new verb sibling to mantle/charter/knight/forfeit.
Distribution dissolves (nothing to ship);
revocation becomes one IAM binding deletion or OAuth consent revocation;
audit logs gain `delegationInfo` attributing actions to the human behind the SA.
Additive in the near term — RBRA keyfiles remain necessary for headless paths (Cloud Build itself, automated fixtures, Ifrit-style scenarios), and a regime mode field selects per-role credential mode.
Open questions remain (storage location, OAuth client scope, probe coexistence) — see memo for full design.
_Trigger_: first concrete external-user request for browser-based onboarding, or first time keyfile distribution materially blocks adoption rather than being theoretically frictionful.
_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260427-google-native-human-auth.md`.
