content.content: /Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/README.md

1 # Recipe Bottle 2 3 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) provides two independent container image capabilities: 4 5 - **[Foundry](#Foundry)**: orchestrate Google Cloud Build to produce multiplatform container images (x86 + ARM), fetch, retain, and serve them using a role-managed private cloud registry — with optional [egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) builds and supply-chain [provenance](#Provenance) 6 - **[Crucible](#Crucible)**: run untrusted containers behind enforced network isolation — DNS filtering and IP filtering — even using images unmodified from "in the wild" 7 8 > [!IMPORTANT] 9 > **Early-stage project — security review welcome in both domains** 10 > 11 > The [Foundry's](#Foundry) egress-locked Cloud Build configuration — including the [SLSA](#Provenance) attestation chain, [build isolation](#BuildIsolation), and digest-pinned toolchains — has not yet had broad independent review. 12 > 13 > The [Crucible](#Crucible) runtime containment — a multi-container apparatus where the workload runs unprivileged in a network namespace it does not control — has also not had broad review, particularly the network isolation rules, privileged namespace setup, and egress enforcement. 14 > 15 > If you evaluate or deploy this, you are contributing to its hardening. 16 > Security-focused contributors and responsible disclosure are especially valued. 17 18 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is a set of bash scripts enabling enterprise grade container image management intended for incorporation into any project. 19 The dependency footprint is deliberately narrow — `bash 3.2` and a handful of standard tools — with no Python runtime, no language-specific package manager, and no `gcloud` CLI. 20 After initial manual setup, all cloud API calls use `openssl` + `curl`. 21 A small team can stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without specialized DevOps expertise. 22 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) goal is a workflow where every container image has a verified origin and a controlled version, running behind appropriate network safeguards. 23 24 **Project page**: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle 25 26

27 28

29 30 ## Environment 31 32 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is organized around two independent capabilities: the [Foundry](#Foundry) builds container images with verifiable [provenance](#Provenance), and the [Crucible](#Crucible) runs untrusted images with enforced network isolation. 33 The two compose but neither requires the other. 34 35 ### Supported Platforms 36 37 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) [Crucible](#Crucible) runtime is qualified for release-1 against **Docker** as the container runtime, on three host families: 38 39 - **Linux host** — native Docker Engine (Docker CE / `docker.io`) running directly on the host kernel; no VM. Tested on Ubuntu LTS with cgroup v2 and a 6.x kernel. 40 - **Windows host** — Docker Desktop for Windows with the WSL2 backend, and per-distro WSL integration enabled for the Recipe Bottle WSL distribution. Single-daemon model: Docker Desktop's daemon serves all shells (Windows, Cygwin, WSL) via the default Docker context. 41 - **macOS host** — Docker Desktop for Mac on a supported macOS release. Apple Silicon hosts use the Apple Virtualization framework or Docker VMM hypervisor backend. 42 43 Podman support is architecturally accommodated by the spec but deferred — see §Future Work. 44 45 All configuration flows through [Regimes](#Regime) — structured `.env` files with typed validation, each with its own render and validate commands. 46 Some regimes are committed in the repo: [Vessel](#Vessel) definitions ([RBRV](#RBRV)), [Nameplate](#Nameplate) configurations ([RBRN](#RBRN)), [Depot](#Depot) identity ([RBRR](#RBRR)), and [Payor](#Payor) identity ([RBRP](#RBRP)). 47 Others live on the filesystem outside revision control: OAuth credentials ([RBRO](#RBRO)), role credentials ([RBRA](#RBRA)), and developer workstation paths ([BURS](#BURS)). 48 49 Every operation is launched through a [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) — a shell script in the `tt/` directory. 50 The critical property: tab completion finds the command you want. 51 Type `tt/rbw-` and the shell narrows to all [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) operations; type `tt/rbw-h` to see just the [Hallmark](#Hallmark) commands. 52 Each [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) is named `{colophon}.{frontispiece}.sh` — the colophon routes to the right module, the frontispiece tells you what it does. 53 54 Every state-changing [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) writes three [Log](#Log) files to the directory named by `BURS_LOG_DIR` in your [BURS](#BURS) station file: a stable-name file (always the same path — easy for tooling to locate and evaluate the most recent run), a per-command file (same command name across runs — tools like SlickEdit sense diffs between executions), and a timestamped historical file (permanent record). 55 Disk space is cheap; [Log](#Log) unconditionally so the diagnostic evidence is always there when something fails. 56 Handbooks don't [Log](#Log) — teaching output is ephemeral. 57 58 A [Transcript](#Transcript) is a single file capturing key decision points and state transitions within a [Tabtarget's](#Tabtarget) execution. 59 Where [Logs](#Log) preserve full terminal output, a [Transcript](#Transcript) records the structured progress of sophisticated orchestration commands — the first thing to read when debugging a multi-step failure. 60 61 The [Output](#Output) directory is a fixed-path staging area cleared and recreated before each [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) runs. 62 Commands that produce artifacts write them here. 63 Concurrent bash sessions share this path, so parallel commands can overwrite each other's [Output](#Output). 64 65 To begin, run the onboarding walkthrough: 66 67 ``` 68 tt/rbw-o.ONBOARDING.sh 69 ``` 70 71 ## Foundry 72 73 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) remote build orchestration system for producing, attesting, and distributing container images via Google Cloud Build and Google Artifact Registry. 74 The [Foundry](#Foundry) manages [Depot](#Depot) access, [Vessels](#Vessel) choreography, [Hallmark](#Hallmark) tracking, and build definitions. 75 Three [Vessel](#Vessel) modes determine how images enter the [Depot](#Depot): [Conjure](#Conjure) ([egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) build from source with [SLSA provenance](#Provenance)), [Bind](#Bind) (digest-pinned upstream mirror), and [Graft](#Graft) (local push). 76 Peer to [Crucible](#Crucible), which handles local runtime containment. 77 78 The [Foundry](#Foundry) orchestrates Google Cloud Build to produce container images with [SLSA](#Provenance) attestation, software bills of material, reproducible multi-architecture builds, and digest-pinned toolchains — so every image has a verifiable origin story. 79 Builds run in an [egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) configuration, drawing from upstream base images mirrored into a project-owned [Depot](#Depot) registry — a fixed, self-contained supply chain independent of third-party registry availability. 80 81 ### Depot 82 83 The facility where container images are built and stored — has its own GCP project with an artifact registry and a storage bucket, funded under the [Manor's](#Manor) billing account. 84 The [Payor](#Payor) [Levies](#Levy) a [Depot](#Depot), and the [Governor](#Governor) administers access to it. 85 Each [Depot](#Depot) operates as an independent supply-chain boundary with its own credentials, builds, and registry. 86 87 Each [Depot](#Depot) supports two build egress profiles: 88 89 - **[Tethered](#Tethered)** — Build egress mode allowing public internet access during Cloud Build. [Tethered](#Tethered) builds pull base images from upstream registries at build time — simpler to set up, but dependent on upstream availability. 90 - **[Airgap](#Airgap)** — Build egress mode with no public internet access during Cloud Build. 91 [Airgap](#Airgap) builds draw all dependencies from [Enshrined](#Enshrine) images in the [Depot's](#Depot) registry — fully self-contained, independent of upstream availability. 92 Requires [Enshrining](#Enshrine) base images before the first build. 93 See [Build Isolation](#BuildIsolation) for the security rationale behind these profiles. 94 95 ### Manor 96 97 The [Payor's](#Payor) administrative seat — holds the billing account, OAuth client, and operator identity. 98 [Depot](#Depot) projects are created and funded under the [Manor's](#Manor) authority. 99 The [Manor](#Manor) has its own GCP project, distinct from any [Depot](#Depot) project. 100 101 ### Payor 102 103 [Establishes](#Establish) a [Manor](#Manor) and funds [Depot](#Depot) projects through it; authenticates via OAuth. 104 The [Payor](#Payor) is the only role requiring manual Google Cloud Console interaction — [Establishing](#Establish) the [Manor](#Manor), configuring OAuth, and [Installing](#Install) credentials via browser flow. 105 All other roles descend from credentials the [Payor's](#Payor) infrastructure creates. 106 107 ### Governor 108 109 Administers a [Depot](#Depot): creates service accounts, manages access. 110 The [Governor](#Governor) is [Mantled](#Mantle) by the [Payor](#Payor) and holds the administrative credential for the [Depot](#Depot). 111 The [Governor](#Governor) [Invests](#Invest) [Directors](#Director) and [Retrievers](#Retriever) with credentials, [Divests](#Divest) credentials no longer needed, and [Rosters](#Roster) issued credentials. 112 113 ### Director 114 115 Builds and publishes [Vessel](#Vessel) images into a [Depot](#Depot). 116 Each [Director](#Director) credential is scoped to one [Depot](#Depot). 117 The [Director](#Director) manages the image lifecycle: [Ordain](#Ordain) a [Hallmark](#Hallmark), [Tally](#Tally) registry health, [Rekon](#Rekon) raw tags, [Vouch](#Vouch) [provenance](#Provenance), [Abjure](#Abjure) superseded artifacts, and [Jettison](#Jettison) individual tags. 118 119 ### Retriever 120 121 Pulls and runs [Vessel](#Vessel) images from a [Depot](#Depot). 122 This is the most constrained role — read-only access to the [Depot](#Depot) registry. 123 The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images for local use, [Plumbs](#Plumb) their [provenance](#Provenance), or [Wrests](#Wrest) a specific image directly. 124 125 ### Vessel 126 127 A specification for a container image — built from source ([Conjure](#Conjure)), mirrored from upstream ([Bind](#Bind)), or pushed from local ([Graft](#Graft)). 128 Each [Vessel](#Vessel) is a directory under `rbev-vessels/` containing at minimum an `rbrv.env` configuration file; [Conjure](#Conjure) [Vessels](#Vessel) also include a Dockerfile. 129 A fourth mode, [Kludge](#Kludge), builds locally for development without involving the [Depot](#Depot). 130 131 ### Hallmark 132 133 A specific build instance of a [Vessel](#Vessel), identified by timestamp. 134 [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) are the unit of [provenance](#Provenance) tracking — each one records when and how the image was produced. 135 Each [Hallmark](#Hallmark) produces three tagged artifacts in the [Depot](#Depot) registry: the container image (`-image`), the [software bill of materials](#SBOM) ([`-about`](#About)), and the cryptographic attestation ([`-vouch`](#Vouch)). 136 [Hallmark](#Hallmark) values are recorded into [Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Regime](#Regime) files to pin a [Nameplate](#Nameplate) to specific image versions. 137 138 ### Foundry Lifecycle 139 140 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) uses a role-based security model with four roles, each building on the previous: 141 142 | Role | Authenticates via | Purpose | 143 |------|-------------------|---------| 144 | [**Payor**](#Payor) | OAuth (browser flow) | Creates/funds GCP infrastructure, manages [Governor](#Governor) lifecycle | 145 | [**Governor**](#Governor) | Service account credential | Administers [Director](#Director) and [Retriever](#Retriever) credentials within a [Depot](#Depot) | 146 | [**Director**](#Director) | Service account credential | Submits builds, manages images, verifies [provenance](#Provenance) | 147 | [**Retriever**](#Retriever) | Service account credential | Pulls images for local use | 148 149 The [Payor](#Payor) stands apart — it requires manual Google Cloud Console work and OAuth authentication. 150 All downstream roles authenticate via credential files, enabling full automation. 151 152 #### Establishment and Provisioning 153 154 The [Payor](#Payor) begins by [Establishing](#Establish) a GCP project and configuring an OAuth consent screen through the Google Cloud Console. 155 After downloading the OAuth client credentials, the [Payor](#Payor) [Installs](#Install) them via a browser authorization flow — the resulting refresh token is stored locally with restrictive permissions and does not need to be repeated. 156 157 With [Payor](#Payor) credentials in place, the [Payor](#Payor) [Levies](#Levy) a [Depot](#Depot), provisioning it with build infrastructure, artifact registry, and secrets storage. 158 The [Payor](#Payor) then [Mantles](#Mantle) a [Governor](#Governor) service account to administer the [Depot](#Depot). 159 160 Before the first build can run, the [Depot](#Depot) needs its supply-chain infrastructure in place: upstream base images must be [Enshrined](#Enshrine) into the registry, and a [Reliquary](#Reliquary) of builder tool images must be inscribed. 161 162 #### Credential Distribution 163 164 The [Governor](#Governor) [Invests](#Invest) downstream credentials: a [Director](#Director) for build operations and a [Retriever](#Retriever) for image pull access. 165 Each credential is scoped to a single role within a single [Depot](#Depot). 166 167 #### Build and Retrieve 168 169 The [Director](#Director) [Ordains](#Ordain) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) for each [Vessel](#Vessel) — [Conjuring](#Conjure) from source, [Binding](#Bind) from upstream, or [Grafting](#Graft) from local builds. 170 After builds complete, the [Director](#Director) [Tallies](#Tally) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) by health status and [Vouches](#Vouch) their [provenance](#Provenance). 171 [Hallmark](#Hallmark) values from the [Tally](#Tally) are recorded into [Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Regime](#Regime) files, completing the chain from build to runtime. 172 173 The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images locally for use. 174 175 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) builds container images on Google Cloud Build (GCB) and stores them in Google Artifact Registry (GAR): 176 177 - Isolated build environments using Google-curated Cloud Build builder images 178 - Multi-architecture support via `docker buildx` with binfmt emulation 179 - [SLSA provenance](#Provenance) attestation and verification 180 - [Software Bills of Material (SBOM)](#SBOM) for every build 181 - Full build transcripts captured as auxiliary metadata artifacts 182 - Upstream base images [Enshrined](#Enshrine) into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry, so builds do not depend on third-party registry availability at build time 183 - `gcloud` never runs on the workstation — REST calls via `curl` and `jq` drive all remote operations, and the Google-supplied `gcloud` binary is confined to Cloud Build step containers on the server side 184 185 Each build's source context is packaged as a [Pouch](#Pouch) — the security boundary between workstation and build infrastructure. 186 187 ## Crucible 188 189 The distinctive case [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) addresses is *running untrusted code*: third-party tooling, experimental packages, binaries with uncertain [provenance](#Provenance). 190 Containers excel at packaging known applications, but running unvetted code poses security risks that ordinary container deployment does not solve. 191 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) assembles a [Crucible](#Crucible) — three cooperating containers where a [Sentry](#Sentry) enforces network policy — without requiring modifications to existing container images. 192 The [Bottle](#Bottle) container runs unmodified, in a network namespace prepared by a privileged [Pentacle](#Pentacle), with all egress flowing through the [Sentry](#Sentry) gateway. 193 194 The [Sentry](#Sentry)/[Pentacle](#Pentacle)/[Bottle](#Bottle) triad running together as one unit defined by a [Nameplate](#Nameplate). 195 The [Crucible](#Crucible) is the local safety orchestration — the apparatus that makes running untrusted code practical. 196 [Charging](#Charge) starts all three containers; [Quenching](#Quench) stops and cleans them up. 197 198 ### Nameplate 199 200 Per-[Vessel](#Vessel) configuration tying a [Sentry](#Sentry) and [Bottle](#Bottle) together into a runnable [Crucible](#Crucible). 201 The [Nameplate](#Nameplate) moniker (e.g. `tadmor`) identifies the unit across all operations. 202 Each [Nameplate](#Nameplate) declares its [Vessel](#Vessel) selections, [Hallmark](#Hallmark) pins, and the network policy that the [Sentry](#Sentry) enforces. 203 204 ### Containers 205 206 - **[Sentry](#Sentry)** — Security container enforcing network policies via `iptables` and `dnsmasq`. 207 The [Sentry](#Sentry) applies two layers of egress policy: DNS-level filtering (only allowed domain names resolve) and IP-level filtering (only allowed CIDR ranges pass). 208 A compromised [Bottle](#Bottle) cannot bypass either layer — the [Sentry](#Sentry) is the sole gateway between the [Bottle](#Bottle) and the outside network. 209 - **[Pentacle](#Pentacle)** — Privileged container establishing the network namespace shared with the [Bottle](#Bottle). 210 The [Pentacle](#Pentacle) runs briefly with elevated privileges to create the network topology, then remains as the namespace anchor. 211 Security policies are enforced from the first packet because the [Sentry](#Sentry) configures the namespace before the [Bottle](#Bottle) starts. 212 - **[Bottle](#Bottle)** — Your workload container, running unmodified in a controlled network environment. 213 The [Bottle](#Bottle) has no direct network access — all traffic routes through the [Sentry](#Sentry) gateway in a namespace prepared by the [Pentacle](#Pentacle). 214 Any existing container image can run as a [Bottle](#Bottle) without modification. 215 216 ### Enclave 217 218 The isolated network connecting a [Bottle](#Bottle) to its [Sentry](#Sentry) — the [Bottle's](#Bottle) only path to the outside world. 219 All [Bottle](#Bottle) traffic routes through the [Enclave](#Enclave) to the [Sentry](#Sentry) gateway; the [Bottle](#Bottle) has no interface on any other network. 220 221 ### Crucible Lifecycle 222 223 [Charge](#Charge) the [Crucible](#Crucible) for a [Nameplate](#Nameplate) to start the [Sentry](#Sentry), [Pentacle](#Pentacle), and [Bottle](#Bottle) together — the [Bottle](#Bottle) is ready for interactive use immediately. 224 [Rack](#Rack) the [Bottle](#Bottle) to shell in, [Hail](#Hail) the [Sentry](#Sentry) to inspect the gateway, or [Scry](#Scry) the network to observe traffic across [Crucible](#Crucible) containers. 225 When finished, [Quench](#Quench) the [Crucible](#Crucible) to stop and clean up all three containers. 226 To inspect an image's supply chain, [Plumb](#Plumb) its [provenance](#Provenance) — the full view shows the [SBOM](#SBOM), build info, and Dockerfile; the compact view summarizes the attestation chain. 227 228 ### Reference Nameplates 229 230 Shipped [Nameplates](#Nameplate) demonstrating different [Crucible](#Crucible) configurations. 231 Each pairs a [Sentry](#Sentry) with a [Bottle](#Bottle) [Vessel](#Vessel) and defines the network policy for that deployment target. 232 233 **[ccyolo](#ccyolo)** — Claude Code sandbox for network-contained AI development. 234 The [ccyolo](#ccyolo) [Nameplate](#Nameplate) pairs the [Sentry](#Sentry) with a Claude Code [Bottle](#Bottle) under an Anthropic-only network allowlist — SSH entry from the workstation, OAuth authentication via copy/paste, everything else blocked. 235 [Kludge](#Kludge)-only development target: no cloud account, no service account credentials, fully self-contained on the developer's workstation. 236 The onboarding handbook's first hands-on track teaches the full [Crucible](#Crucible) lifecycle using [ccyolo](#ccyolo). 237 238 **[tadmor](#tadmor)** — Adversarial security testing [Nameplate](#Nameplate) for daily iteration. 239 The [tadmor](#tadmor) [Nameplate](#Nameplate) pairs the [Sentry](#Sentry) with the [Ifrit](#Ifrit) attack [Vessel](#Vessel) under a restrictive network allowlist, consuming [Kludged](#Kludge) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) for fast author-test-iterate cycles. 240 The [Theurge](#Theurge) test orchestrator [Charges](#Charge) [tadmor](#tadmor) and dispatches curated escape attempts to validate that the [Sentry's](#Sentry) containment holds under adversarial conditions. 241 242 **[moriah](#moriah)** — Adversarial security testing [Nameplate](#Nameplate) for the airgap supply chain. 243 The [moriah](#moriah) [Nameplate](#Nameplate) pairs the [Sentry](#Sentry) with the [Ifrit](#Ifrit) attack [Vessel](#Vessel) under the same restrictive network allowlist as [tadmor](#tadmor), consuming [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) [Ordained](#Ordain) end-to-end on the [Airgap](#Airgap) pool. 244 The [Theurge](#Theurge) runs the same escape attempts against [moriah](#moriah) as against [tadmor](#tadmor) — the cloud-built variant validating that containment holds identically when the supply chain produces the inputs. 245 246 ## Release Procedure 247 248 The project maintainer release qualification ceremony — five operator steps, roughly one hour wall-clock, with cloud cost on the order of two GCP projects per run. 249 See [RELEASE.md](RELEASE.md) for the full procedure. 250 251 ## Appendix: Foundry Operations 252 253 Formal definitions for all [Foundry](#Foundry) operations, organized by lifecycle phase. 254 255 ### Infrastructure 256 257 **[Establish](#Establish)** — Guided setup of a new [Manor](#Manor) — creates the [Manor's](#Manor) GCP project and configures the OAuth consent screen through the Google Cloud Console. 258 [Establishing](#Establish) walks the [Payor](#Payor) through project creation, API enablement, and consent screen configuration — the manual prerequisites before any automated operations can run. 259 260 **[Install](#Install)** — Ingest OAuth client credentials from a downloaded JSON key file. 261 [Installing](#Install) triggers a browser authorization flow and stores the resulting refresh token locally with restrictive permissions (`600`). 262 This is a one-time [Payor](#Payor) operation — the refresh token persists until explicitly revoked. 263 264 **[Levy](#Levy)** — Provision a new [Depot's](#Depot) GCP infrastructure. 265 [Levying](#Levy) creates the GCP project, artifact registry, storage bucket, and build configuration. 266 This is a [Payor](#Payor) operation that binds [Regime](#Regime) configuration to real cloud resources. 267 268 **[Unmake](#Unmake)** — Permanently destroy a [Depot's](#Depot) GCP infrastructure — project, artifact registry, storage bucket, and all contents. 269 [Unmaking](#Unmake) is the reverse of [Levying](#Levy). 270 This is a [Payor](#Payor) operation and is irreversible. 271 272 **[Refresh](#Refresh)** — Refresh an expired [Payor](#Payor) OAuth token. 273 OAuth tokens expire periodically; [Refreshing](#Refresh) re-authenticates via the stored refresh token without repeating the full [Install](#Install) flow. 274 Run when [Payor](#Payor) operations fail with authentication errors. 275 276 **[Quota](#Quota)** — Review Cloud Build capacity and usage. 277 [Quota](#Quota) displays the current build minute allocation, consumption, and any throttling in effect for the [Depot's](#Depot) GCP project. 278 279 ### Credentials 280 281 **[Mantle](#Mantle)** — Create or replace the [Governor](#Governor) service account for a [Depot](#Depot). 282 [Mantling](#Mantle) is a [Payor](#Payor) operation that provisions the administrative credential — the [Governor](#Governor) inherits the [Payor's](#Payor) authority to manage the [Depot](#Depot) but authenticates via service account key rather than OAuth. 283 284 **[Invest](#Invest)** — Create a [Director](#Director) or [Retriever](#Retriever) service account. 285 [Investing](#Invest) provisions a new credential scoped to a single role within a single [Depot](#Depot) and emits its credential file in one step. 286 This is a [Governor](#Governor) operation; investing fails if the named credential already exists, so re-keying requires [Divesting](#Divest) first. 287 288 **[Divest](#Divest)** — Revoke a [Director](#Director) or [Retriever](#Retriever) service account. 289 [Divesting](#Divest) deletes the service account and removes the local credential file — the credential becomes permanently unusable. 290 This is a [Governor](#Governor) operation used when a credential is compromised, no longer needed, or being rotated. 291 292 **[Roster](#Roster)** — Inventory [Director](#Director) or [Retriever](#Retriever) service accounts within a [Depot](#Depot). 293 [Rostering](#Roster) shows credentials issued under each role with their creation dates and status. 294 This is a [Governor](#Governor) operation; observation-only, no cloud mutation. 295 296 ### Supply Chain 297 298 **[Enshrine](#Enshrine)** — Mirror upstream base images into your [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 299 [Enshrining](#Enshrine) ensures the build pipeline has a fixed, self-contained supply chain — builds draw from project-owned copies rather than depending on third-party registry availability at build time. 300 301 **[Reliquary](#Reliquary)** — Co-versioned set of builder tool images (skopeo, docker, gcloud, syft) inscribed from upstream into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 302 Cloud Build jobs use [Reliquary](#Reliquary) images as step containers, ensuring builds run with known, project-owned toolchains rather than pulling tools from upstream at build time. 303 The [Director](#Director) inscribes a [Reliquary](#Reliquary) before any [Ordain](#Ordain) or [Enshrine](#Enshrine) operation can run. 304 305 ### Building 306 307 **[Ordain](#Ordain)** — Create a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) with full attestation — the production build operation. 308 [Ordaining](#Ordain) is mode-aware: it [Conjures](#Conjure), [Binds](#Bind), or [Grafts](#Graft) depending on the [Vessel's](#Vessel) configuration. 309 Each [Ordain](#Ordain) produces an image in the [Depot](#Depot) registry with associated [provenance](#Provenance) metadata. 310 311 **[Conjure](#Conjure)** — Cloud Build creates the image from source. 312 [Conjure](#Conjure) builds run in an [egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) environment with digest-pinned toolchains, producing full [SLSA](#Provenance) attestation and [SBOMs](#SBOM). 313 This is the highest-trust build mode. 314 315 **[Bind](#Bind)** — Mirror an upstream image pinned by digest. 316 [Binding](#Bind) captures an external image at a specific digest into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 317 Trust is established through digest-pin verification rather than build [provenance](#Provenance). 318 319 **[Graft](#Graft)** — Push a locally-built image to the [Depot](#Depot) registry. 320 [Grafting](#Graft) uploads a local image to GAR via docker push — no Cloud Build for the image itself, though [About](#About) and [Vouch](#Vouch) metadata still run in Cloud Build. 321 This is the lowest-trust mode (GRAFTED verdict). 322 323 **[Kludge](#Kludge)** — Build a [Vessel](#Vessel) image locally for fast iteration, without [Depot](#Depot) registry push. 324 [Kludging](#Kludge) produces a local Docker image for development and testing without involving Cloud Build or the [Depot](#Depot). 325 The resulting image can be used to [Charge](#Charge) a [Crucible](#Crucible) directly. 326 327 **[Pouch](#Pouch)** — Build context packaged as a FROM SCRATCH OCI image and pushed to the [Depot's](#Depot) registry before a Cloud Build job runs. 328 The [Director](#Director) controls what enters the [Pouch](#Pouch) — Dockerfile, context files, build scripts — and the cloud receives only what the [Pouch](#Pouch) contains. 329 This is the security boundary between workstation and build infrastructure. 330 331 ### Verification 332 333 **[Tally](#Tally)** — Inventory [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) in the [Depot](#Depot) registry by health status. 334 [Tallying](#Tally) shows which builds succeeded, which are pending, and which failed. 335 The [Director](#Director) [Tallies](#Tally) before [Vouching](#Vouch) to confirm build completion. 336 337 **[Rekon](#Rekon)** — Raw listing of image tags in the [Depot](#Depot) registry for a [Vessel](#Vessel) package. 338 [Rekon](#Rekon) is a [Director](#Director)-only diagnostic that shows exactly what exists in the registry without health interpretation. 339 Where [Tally](#Tally) groups [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) by status, [Rekon](#Rekon) shows the unprocessed tag inventory. 340 341 **[Vouch](#Vouch)** — Cryptographic attestation proving a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) was built by trusted infrastructure. 342 The [Vouch](#Vouch) verdict is mode-aware: [Conjure](#Conjure) builds receive full [SLSA provenance](#Provenance) verification, [Bind](#Bind) builds receive digest-pin verification, and [Graft](#Graft) builds receive a GRAFTED verdict with no [provenance](#Provenance) chain. 343 The [Director](#Director) [Vouches](#Vouch) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) after [Tallying](#Tally) their build status. 344 345 **[About](#About)** — Build metadata and [software bill of materials](#SBOM) for a [Hallmark](#Hallmark). 346 The [About](#About) artifact (`-about` tag) contains the [SBOM](#SBOM), build transcript, build configuration snapshot, and key package summaries — bundled as a compressed archive and stored as a Generic Artifact in GAR. 347 Every [Ordain](#Ordain) produces an [About](#About) alongside the image. 348 349 **[Plumb](#Plumb)** — Inspect an artifact's [provenance](#Provenance) — [SBOM](#SBOM), build info, and [Vouch](#Vouch) chain. 350 [Plumbing](#Plumb) provides full transparency into how an image was built and what it contains. 351 Two views are available: full ([SBOM](#SBOM), build info, Dockerfile) and compact (attestation summary). 352 353 ### Distribution 354 355 **[Summon](#Summon)** — Pull a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) image from the [Depot](#Depot) to your local machine. 356 The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images for local use — the final step before a [Hallmark's](#Hallmark) image can be used in a [Crucible](#Crucible). 357 358 **[Wrest](#Wrest)** — Pull a specific image from the [Depot](#Depot) registry by reference. 359 [Wresting](#Wrest) is a direct pull without [Vouch](#Vouch) verification — used when you need a specific image tag regardless of attestation status. 360 Compare with [Summon](#Summon), which enforces the [Vouch](#Vouch) ceremony. 361 362 ### Removal 363 364 **[Abjure](#Abjure)** — Remove a [Hallmark's](#Hallmark) artifacts from the [Depot's](#Depot) registry — the `-image`, [`-about`](#About), and [`-vouch`](#Vouch) tags deleted as a coherent unit. 365 [Abjuring](#Abjure) is the reverse of [Ordaining](#Ordain): it formally renounces a build instance. 366 The [Director](#Director) [Abjures](#Abjure) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) that are superseded, broken, or no longer needed. 367 368 **[Jettison](#Jettison)** — Delete a specific image tag from the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 369 [Jettisoning](#Jettison) is lower-level than [Abjure](#Abjure) — it removes a single tag rather than a complete [Hallmark](#Hallmark) artifact set. 370 Used for cleanup of individual registry entries. 371 372 ### Diagnostics 373 374 **[List Depots](#ListDepots)** — Inventory all active [Depots](#Depot) visible to the current [Payor](#Payor) credentials. 375 Shows project IDs, regions, and provisioning status. 376 377 **[JWT Probe](#JWTProbe)** — Test service account authentication. 378 The [JWT Probe](#JWTProbe) verifies that a [Governor](#Governor), [Director](#Director), or [Retriever](#Retriever) credential can successfully authenticate to the [Depot's](#Depot) GCP project — useful for diagnosing access failures after credential creation or rotation. 379 380 **[OAuth Probe](#OAuthProbe)** — Test [Payor](#Payor) OAuth authentication. 381 The [OAuth Probe](#OAuthProbe) verifies that the stored refresh token can obtain a valid access token — useful for diagnosing [Payor](#Payor) operation failures before attempting a full [Refresh](#Refresh). 382 383 ## Appendix: Crucible Operations 384 385 Formal definitions for all [Crucible](#Crucible) operations. 386 387 ### Lifecycle 388 389 **[Charge](#Charge)** — Start a [Crucible](#Crucible) — the [Sentry](#Sentry)/[Pentacle](#Pentacle)/[Bottle](#Bottle) triad — defined by a [Nameplate](#Nameplate). 390 [Charging](#Charge) brings up the [Crucible](#Crucible) in dependency order: [Pentacle](#Pentacle) creates the namespace, [Sentry](#Sentry) configures policy, then the [Bottle](#Bottle) starts with its network already constrained. 391 392 **[Quench](#Quench)** — Stop and clean up a [Charged](#Charge) [Crucible's](#Crucible) containers. 393 [Quenching](#Quench) tears down the [Crucible](#Crucible) in reverse order and removes the network resources created during [Charging](#Charge). 394 395 ### Interaction 396 397 **[Rack](#Rack)** — Shell into a [Bottle](#Bottle) container. 398 [Racking](#Rack) opens an interactive session inside the running workload — for debugging, inspecting state, or running commands as the [Bottle](#Bottle) user would experience them. 399 400 **[Hail](#Hail)** — Shell into a [Sentry](#Sentry) container. 401 [Hailing](#Hail) opens an interactive session on the gateway — for inspecting `iptables` rules, `dnsmasq` configuration, network state, and egress logs. 402 403 **[Scry](#Scry)** — Observe network traffic across [Crucible](#Crucible) containers. 404 [Scrying](#Scry) captures packets on the [Crucible's](#Crucible) network interfaces — for verifying that blocked traffic is actually blocked, diagnosing connectivity issues, or watching the [Sentry's](#Sentry) filtering in action. 405 406 ## Appendix: Adversarial Test Method 407 408 The [Crucible's](#Crucible) containment is validated through coordinated escape testing using two components: 409 410 - **[Ifrit](#Ifrit)** — Adversarial attack [Vessel](#Vessel) purpose-built to run inside a [Bottle](#Bottle), seeking escape. 411 The [Ifrit](#Ifrit) carries scapy (arbitrary packet construction), strace (syscall boundary probing), and a minimal footprint — tools chosen to probe every surface the [Sentry's](#Sentry) containment exposes. 412 Named for the djinn imprisoned in a bottle. 413 - **[Theurge](#Theurge)** — Test orchestrator running on the host, outside the [Crucible](#Crucible). 414 The [Theurge](#Theurge) [Charges](#Charge) a [Crucible](#Crucible) with the [Ifrit](#Ifrit) as its [Bottle](#Bottle), then dispatches curated, reproducible, version-controlled attack scripts targeting specific surfaces: DNS exfiltration, ICMP covert channels, cloud metadata probing, namespace breakout, and direct IP bypass attempts. 415 Each attack runs inside the [Bottle](#Bottle) while the [Theurge](#Theurge) simultaneously observes the [Sentry's](#Sentry) network from outside — confirming that blocked traffic is actually blocked, not merely unrequested. 416 417 The escape tests were developed through adversarial Claude Code sessions with full visibility into the [Sentry's](#Sentry) source, configuration, and the [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) specification. 418 The [Ifrit](#Ifrit) [Vessel](#Vessel) is the delivery vehicle; the intelligence came from the authoring process. 419 Every test that passes is evidence the containment holds — not proof. 420 The test suite grows as new attack surfaces are identified. 421 422 ## Appendix: Supply Chain Provenance 423 424 Supply chain provenance is a cryptographically signed record of how a container image was produced — what source, what builder, what steps — so that consumers can verify an image came from trusted infrastructure and was not tampered with in transit or at rest. 425 426 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) achieves [SLSA](https://slsa.dev) v1.0 Build Level 3 for [Conjure](#Conjure) builds, auto-generated by Google Cloud Build. 427 The [Vouch](#Vouch) step independently verifies each build's DSSE envelope signature against Google's attestor public keys from `projects/verified-builder` KMS — using Python standard library and `openssl` only, with no third-party verifier. 428 429 Provenance guarantees are mode-aware: 430 431 | [Vessel](#Vessel) Mode | Trust Basis | [Vouch](#Vouch) Verdict | 432 |------|-------------|------| 433 | [**Conjure**](#Conjure) | Full SLSA v1.0 Level 3 — signed build provenance from GCB | DSSE envelope signature verification | 434 | [**Bind**](#Bind) | Digest-pin comparison — image in GAR matches pinned upstream reference | Digest-pin match | 435 | [**Graft**](#Graft) | Locally built and pushed — no cloud build involvement | GRAFTED (explicit no-provenance marker) | 436 437 Deliberately excluded: no `slsa-verifier` binary, no `gcloud` CLI on the workstation, no `jq` in the verification path. 438 The [Vouch](#Vouch) verifier reconstructs Pre-Authenticated Encoding (PAE), decodes the base64url payload and signature, and verifies via `openssl dgst` against embedded attestor keys — a minimal, auditable trust chain. 439 440 ## Appendix: Software Bill of Materials 441 442 A Software Bill of Materials ([SBOM](#SBOM)) is a machine-readable inventory of every component inside a container image — every OS package, every library, every binary, with versions. 443 Without one, a container image is an opaque filesystem whose contents you discover by running it, which is exactly the wrong time to find out it ships a vulnerable dependency. 444 445 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) generates an [SBOM](#SBOM) for every build using [Syft](https://github.com/anchore/syft), scanning each per-platform image during the [About](#About) assembly step. 446 Each architecture gets its own [SBOM](#SBOM), bundled alongside the build transcript and configuration snapshot in the `-about` artifact stored as a Generic Artifact in GAR. 447 448 An [SBOM](#SBOM) enables three hygiene practices that opaque images cannot support: 449 450 - **CVE triage before deployment** — when a vulnerability is announced, search your [SBOMs](#SBOM) rather than scanning running containers 451 - **Pre-deployment audit** — know what you are granting network access to before a [Crucible](#Crucible) is [Charged](#Charge) 452 - **Build-over-build drift detection** — compare [SBOMs](#SBOM) across [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) to see what changed between builds 453 454 The [Plumb](#Plumb) command surfaces [SBOM](#SBOM) contents: the full view shows package inventories; the compact view summarizes key components. 455 456 ## Appendix: Build Isolation 457 458 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) supports two build egress profiles — [Tethered](#Tethered) and [Airgap](#Airgap) — that determine whether a Cloud Build job can reach the public internet. 459 The distinction is not primarily about availability; it is a security boundary that controls what can enter and exit the build environment. 460 461 **What [Airgap](#Airgap) protects: exfiltration and supply chain injection.** 462 If a compromised dependency, build plugin, or Dockerfile instruction executes during your build, an [Airgapped](#Airgap) build cannot phone home — it cannot transmit source code, secrets, or intermediate artifacts to an external endpoint, and it cannot silently fetch malicious payloads. 463 This is defense-in-depth for proprietary code: even if a build step is compromised, the network is not available as an exfiltration channel. 464 465 **The curated gate principle.** 466 [Airgap](#Airgap) does not mean "nothing external." It means all external content enters through a single auditable gate — the [Enshrine](#Enshrine) ceremony — rather than ad-hoc network fetches during build. 467 The attack surface collapses from "any URL a Dockerfile mentions" to "the specific digests the [Director](#Director) [Enshrined](#Enshrine)." 468 Builder tool images enter through a parallel gate: the [Reliquary](#Reliquary), inscribed once and pinned by digest for all subsequent builds. 469 470 **What [Airgap](#Airgap) does not protect: the base image contents.** 471 Base images like `debian-slim` were themselves built with full internet access — `apt-get install` already ran inside them. 472 The [Airgap](#Airgap) protects *your* build steps on top of those bases, not the base image contents themselves. 473 Base images are vetted separately: digest-pinned at [Enshrine](#Enshrine) time, inspectable via [SBOM](#SBOM), and stored as project-owned copies in the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 474 A [Tethered](#Tethered) build of the base image followed by an [Airgapped](#Airgap) build of your application is the expected pattern — the base image is a known input, your proprietary layers are the protected output. 475 476 **Regulatory alignment.** 477 No framework mandates build-time network blocking by name, but egress-locked builds are the simplest way to evidence several common controls: FedRAMP CM-7 (least functionality) and SC-7 (boundary protection), SOC 2 CC6.1 (logical access) and CC8.1 (change management), and SLSA Level 3's hermetic build requirement. 478 479 ## Appendix: Roadmap 480 481 The following features are not yet implemented but are under consideration: 482 483 - **Crucible conduit for cloud services** - Encrypted tunnel from the [Sentry](#Sentry) to a VPC hosting PrivateLink endpoints, enabling [Bottles](#Bottle) to reach cloud AI services (AWS Bedrock, Vertex AI, Azure OpenAI) without exposing floating cloud IP ranges in the CIDR allowlist. 484 WireGuard terminated at the [Sentry](#Sentry) replaces per-service IP tracking with a single stable VPC CIDR. 485 Near-term, allowlist-only [Nameplates](#Nameplate) targeting specific service CIDRs and domains work today with existing [Sentry](#Sentry) machinery. 486 The tunnel adds defense-in-depth for PrivateLink-capable services; SaaS endpoints without PrivateLink (GitHub.com, GitLab.com) remain served by CIDR/domain allowlisting. 487 488 - **Credential confinement** - Move cloud service credentials (API keys, IAM keys, SSH keys) from the operator's workstation into the [Crucible](#Crucible), injected via [Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Regime](#Regime) configuration. 489 The workstation [Charges](#Charge) the [Crucible](#Crucible) but never holds service credentials — reducing the attack surface from "everything on the workstation" to "one minimal container." 490 Naturally paired with conduit work: credentials and network access are configured together. 491 492 - **VPC Service Controls** - Google Cloud security perimeters that prevent data from being copied out of a project even if an attacker holds valid credentials. 493 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) Cloud Build architecture uses private pools, which are the prerequisite for VPC enforcement; enabling the controls themselves is deferred until organizational policy or external distribution requires them. 494 If a VPC is stood up for the crucible conduit architecture, the VPC-SC perimeter should serve both Cloud Build [egress lockdown](#BuildIsolation) and [Bottle](#Bottle) conduit consumers. 495 496 - **Cosign container signing** - Cryptographic image signatures independent of registry trust. 497 Deferred alongside VPC Service Controls until external distribution triggers the need. 498 499 - **CDN-aware IP gating** - When allowed domains are CDN-hosted (e.g. Cloudflare), the [Sentry's](#Sentry) CIDR allowlist becomes coarse: DNS-level gating remains precise, but IP-level gating is porous across shared CDN address ranges. 500 A tighter mechanism is recognized but not yet designed. 501 502 - **Podman support** - The spec accommodates Podman as an alternative container runtime, but support is deferred. 503 On macOS, both Docker and Podman run Linux containers inside a hidden Linux VM — there is no native container runtime on Darwin. 504 Podman support would require managing that VM's lifecycle within the customer's [Depot](#Depot), adding infrastructure complexity with no architectural advantage over Docker Desktop. 505 506 - **[Crucible](#Crucible)-to-[Crucible](#Crucible) networking** - Under the current [Sentry](#Sentry) model, [Bottles](#Bottle) have no direct network path to each other; any inter-[Bottle](#Bottle) communication would route through their respective [Sentries](#Sentry). 507 The plumbing is feasible but not implemented, pending a concrete use case. 508 509 ## Appendix: Reference Project 510 511 This repository is the reference implementation of [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle). 512 The annotated tree below maps its files to the concepts defined above. 513 514 | Path | Description | 515 |------|-------------| 516 | `Project Root/` | | 517 | `├── CLAUDE.md` | [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) command reference, glossary, conventions | 518 | `├── RELEASE.md` | [Release Procedure](#Release-Procedure) — maintainer release qualification ceremony | 519 | `├── tt/` | 136 [Tabtargets](#Tabtarget) — `tt/rbw-` for all operations | 520 | `├── Tools/` | | 521 | `│ ├── buk/` | Bash Utility Kit — portable CLI infrastructure | 522 | `│ └── rbk/` | Recipe Bottle Kit — domain logic | 523 | `├── .buk/` | [BURC](#BURC) project structure [Regime](#Regime) | 524 | `├── .rbk/` | [Regime](#Regime) configuration root | 525 | `│ ├── rbrp.env` | [RBRP](#RBRP) — [Payor](#Payor) identity for this [Depot](#Depot) | 526 | `│ ├── rbrr.env` | [RBRR](#RBRR) — [Depot](#Depot) identity and build configuration | 527 | `│ ├── ccyolo/` | [Nameplate](#Nameplate) — [ccyolo](#ccyolo) Claude Code sandbox | 528 | `│ │ └── rbrn.env` | [RBRN](#RBRN) — [Sentry](#Sentry) + Claude Code, Anthropic-only allowlist | 529 | `│ ├── tadmor/` | [Nameplate](#Nameplate) — [tadmor](#tadmor) adversarial testing | 530 | `│ │ └── rbrn.env` | [RBRN](#RBRN) — [Sentry](#Sentry) + [Ifrit](#Ifrit), restrictive allowlist | 531 | `│ ├── moriah/` | [Nameplate](#Nameplate) — [moriah](#moriah) airgap-built adversarial testing | 532 | `│ │ └── rbrn.env` | [RBRN](#RBRN) — [Sentry](#Sentry) + [Ifrit](#Ifrit), restrictive allowlist | 533 | `│ ├── srjcl/` | [Nameplate](#Nameplate) — Jupyter notebook | 534 | `│ │ └── rbrn.env` | [RBRN](#RBRN) — [Sentry](#Sentry) + Jupyter, academic-domain allowlist | 535 | `│ └── pluml/` | [Nameplate](#Nameplate) — PlantUML diagram server | 536 | `│ └── rbrn.env` | [RBRN](#RBRN) — [Sentry](#Sentry) + PlantUML, no-egress allowlist | 537 | `└── rbev-vessels/` | [Vessel](#Vessel) definitions | 538 | ` ├── common-sentry-context/` | Shared [Sentry](#Sentry)/[Pentacle](#Pentacle) build context | 539 | ` │ ├── Dockerfile` | debian-slim + iptables + dnsmasq | 540 | ` │ ├── rbjs_sentry.sh` | [Sentry](#Sentry) runtime — policy engine | 541 | ` │ └── rbjp_pentacle.sh` | [Pentacle](#Pentacle) runtime — namespace setup | 542 | ` ├── rbev-sentry-deb-tether/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) — [Sentry](#Sentry) (tethered, upstream pull) | 543 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode, tether egress | 544 | ` ├── rbev-sentry-deb-airgap/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) — [Sentry](#Sentry) (airgapped, enshrined bases) | 545 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode, airgap egress | 546 | ` ├── rbev-bottle-ccyolo/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) — [ccyolo](#ccyolo) Claude Code sandbox | 547 | ` │ ├── Dockerfile` | node:22-slim + SSH + Claude Code | 548 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode | 549 | ` ├── common-ifrit-context/` | Shared [Ifrit](#Ifrit) build context (tether + airgap variants) | 550 | ` │ ├── Dockerfile.tether` | Rust binary + network attack tools (tether build) | 551 | ` │ ├── Dockerfile.airgap` | Same image built `FROM` the forge ([Airgap](#Airgap) build, no upstream pull) | 552 | ` │ ├── Cargo.toml` | Ifrit crate manifest | 553 | ` │ └── src/` | Ifrit source (rbid binary) | 554 | ` ├── common-ifrit-forge-context/` | Build context for the [Ifrit](#Ifrit) forge — warms cargo cache + pre-stages apt deps | 555 | ` │ └── Dockerfile` | Forge image; consumed by `Dockerfile.airgap` as its base | 556 | ` ├── rbev-bottle-ifrit-tether/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) (tether) — [Ifrit](#Ifrit) attack binary | 557 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode, tether egress | 558 | ` ├── rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) ([Airgap](#Airgap)) — [Ifrit](#Ifrit) attack binary, [Ordained](#Ordain) without upstream pull | 559 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode, airgap egress | 560 | ` ├── rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) (tether) — Build-time forge fixture for [Airgap](#Airgap) [Ifrit](#Ifrit) | 561 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode, tether egress | 562 | ` ├── rbev-bottle-plantuml/` | [Bind](#Bind) — upstream image pinned by digest | 563 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Bind](#Bind) mode, digest reference | 564 | ` ├── rbev-bottle-anthropic-jupyter/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) — Jupyter notebook server | 565 | ` │ ├── Dockerfile` | | 566 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode | 567 | ` └── (4 additional test vessels)` | busybox variants for [Theurge](#Theurge) fixture coverage | 568 569 ## Appendix: Specific Regimes 570 571 **[BURC](#BURC)** — Project structure configuration, in the repo. 572 [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) directory, tools directory. 573 574 **[BURS](#BURS)** — Developer workstation configuration. 575 Not in git. 576 Log directory, station paths. 577 578 **[RBRR](#RBRR)** — [Depot](#Depot) identity and build configuration — populated during [Levy](#Levy), consumed by [Director](#Director) and [Retriever](#Retriever) operations. 579 580 **[RBRP](#RBRP)** — [Manor](#Manor) identity — billing account, OAuth client ID, operator email, and the [Manor's](#Manor) GCP project. 581 In the repo. 582 583 **[RBRO](#RBRO)** — [Payor](#Payor) OAuth credentials — client secret and refresh token. 584 Not in the repo. 585 586 **[RBRA](#RBRA)** — Role credentials resident on user workstation, enabling [Governor](#Governor), [Director](#Director), or [Retriever](#Retriever) operations. 587 One credential file per role per [Depot](#Depot). 588 589 **[RBRV](#RBRV)** — [Vessel](#Vessel) configuration specifying [Bind](#Bind), [Conjure](#Conjure), or [Graft](#Graft) mode for creating [Hallmarks](#Hallmark). 590 591 **[RBRN](#RBRN)** — Per-[Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Crucible](#Crucible) configuration mapping two [Vessels](#Vessel) — [Sentry](#Sentry) and [Bottle](#Bottle) — with runtime and [Hallmark](#Hallmark) assignments. 592 593 ## License 594 595 Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc. 596 597 Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0. 598
